1:
"And it came to pass in those days, that there went out a decree from Caesar Augustus, that all the world should be taxed." The witnesses read the census as the providential stage-setting for Christ's birth. The fathers find layers of meaning. Bede: the Son was "begotten in the most peaceful time of the world, because He taught men to seek peace... there could be no greater sign of peace than for the whole world to be brought together under one taxing," and the decree sent every man to his own city so that "the Lord, conceived in one place, born in another, might the more easily escape the fury of the crafty Herod." Ambrose reads "a secular registration, implied a spiritual... a registering of faith: a census of souls," for "the earth is the Lord's." Gregory: "He appeared in the flesh Who should write down the names of His own elect in eternity." Origen sees a sacrament — Christ "placed in the census with the whole world" that "He might impart to the world the communion of Himself."

Several witnesses read the timing as fulfilling Jacob's prophecy. The Greek Exposition: "Christ is born at a time when the princes of Judah had failed... then was fulfilled the prophecy, There shall not fail a leader from Judah... until he shall come who is to be sent." Cyril of Alexandria draws the same from Genesis 49:10, and adds his two-natures Christology: "from the two together is one God the Son, without the diversity of the natures being destroyed by the union... we proclaim the holy Virgin to be the mother of God."

The puritan Henry gathers the marks of "the proper time": Christ "born when the fourth monarchy was in its height" (Daniel 2:44), "when Judea was become a province... tributary," and "when there was now universal peace... the temple of Janus was now shut... fit for the Prince of peace to be born." Of Bethlehem: "the house of bread; a proper place for him to be born in who is the Bread of life," and "the city of David... the Son of David." Providence overruled Augustus's pride: "All the world shall be at the trouble of being enrolled, only that Joseph and Mary may" come to Bethlehem (Micah 5:2).

The grammatical-historical voices supply the history of Augustus and the empire (Gill, Lightfoot, Barnes). The witnesses agree: by an imperial decree of taxation — Augustus serving, unwittingly, the counsel of God — Mary was brought to Bethlehem, that Christ might be born in David's city, in the world's one season of peace, when the sceptre had departed from Judah. The verse displays the providence that bends empires to fulfil prophecy: the Prince of Peace born under the Pax Romana, the Bread of Life in the house of bread, the King of an everlasting kingdom enrolled as a subject of Rome.
2:
"(And this taxing was first made when Cyrenius was governor of Syria.)" This verse poses one of the most discussed historical difficulties in the New Testament, and the grammatical-historical witnesses canvass the solutions. The difficulty, as Barnes states it: "Cyrenius, or Quirinius, was not governor of Syria until twelve or fifteen years after the birth of Jesus. Jesus was born during the reign of Herod... Various attempts have been made to do this." Ellicott lays out the dates: "B.C. 9 Sentius Saturninus; B.C. 6 T. Quintilius Varus; A.D. 6 P. Sulpicius Quirinus."

Several solutions are offered. Lardner's (favoured by Barnes): "This was the first census of Cyrenius, governor of Syria... It is called the first to distinguish it from one afterward taken by Cyrenius (Acts 5:37)... not that he was then governor, but that it was taken by him who was afterward familiarly known as governor" — as one might say "General Washington saved Braddock's army" though not yet a general. A second solution (Clarke, Michaelis) takes the Greek "first" (protos) as "before": "This enrolment was made before Cyrenius was governor of Syria," citing John 1:30. Ellicott judges this "hardly satisfactory," since Luke's manner "is to refer to contemporary events, not to subsequent ones."

The witnesses who write latest, with the benefit of archaeology, vindicate Luke. Robertson: "Ramsay has shown that a periodical fourteen-year census in Egypt is given in dated papyri back to A.D. 20... Ramsay has proven by inscriptions that Quirinius was twice in Syria and that Luke is correct here also." Ellicott reaches the same: Quirinius "was in the East at this time... St. Luke is as accurate in his history here as he is proved to be in all other points... the true meaning is found by emphasising the adjective, This enrolment was the first under Quirinius's government of Syria. He expressly distinguishes it from the more memorable taxing of which Gamaliel speaks (Acts 5:37)." He adds: "Justin Martyr confidently appeals to Roman registers as confirming St. Luke's statement."

The witnesses agree on the substance — Luke parenthetically dates the census to distinguish it from the later, more famous one — while differing on the precise resolution of the chronology: Lardner's "the census Quirinius [the later governor] made," the minority "before Quirinius governed," or (the latest scholarship) "the first under Quirinius's [earlier] governorship," vindicated by inscriptions showing Quirinius twice in Syria. The verse, far from an error, is a "special note of accuracy" (Ellicott) by a historian repeatedly confirmed where he touches the contemporary history of the empire. Luke's careful dating, once thought a stumbling-block, has become a testimony to his reliability.
3:
"And all went to be taxed, every one into his own city." The grammatical-historical witnesses note the Roman custom and its confirmation of Luke's accuracy. Clarke explains the census of Servius Tullius, citing Dionysius of Halicarnassus: each citizen registered "the names of their parents, their own age, the names of their wives and children, adding also what quarter of the city, or what town in the country, they lived in." A Roman census comprised the account of names and the valuation for taxation; this enrolment seems chiefly the former. He compares it to the Domesday Book of William the Conqueror.

Robertson vindicates Luke from the papyri: "A number of papyri in Egypt have the heading enrolment by household (apographe kat' oikian). Here again Luke is vindicated. Each man went to the town where his family register was kept." Ellicott notes the practice was modified in deference to Jewish feeling: "As a rule the practice in a Roman census was to register people in their place of residence; but this was probably modified in Palestine... here again we have not an error, but a special note of accuracy."

The custom of returning to one's ancestral city the witnesses connect to the providence of v.1. Because each went "to his own city," Joseph (of David's house) and Mary were brought to Bethlehem, fulfilling Micah's prophecy. The Roman administrative practice — registration by family and ancestral town — became the means by which the Son of David was born in the city of David.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Roman census required each to register in his ancestral city, a practice confirmed by Egyptian papyri ("enrolment by household") and possibly modified in Palestine in deference to Jewish custom — in either case "a special note of accuracy" (Ellicott) by a careful historian. The verse, far from an error, is a vindicated detail of Roman administration; and the practice of returning to one's family town was the very means of God's providence, bringing Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem that the Son of David might be born in David's city. The empire's bureaucracy, ordering all to their ancestral homes, unwittingly fulfilled the prophecy of Micah 5:2. The verse displays again the harmony of Luke's historical accuracy and the providential design that bent Roman custom to the purposes of God. Each man went where his register was kept; and so the King was registered in Bethlehem.
4:
"And Joseph also went up from Galilee, out of the city of Nazareth, into Judaea, unto the city of David, which is called Bethlehem; (because he was of the house and lineage of David;)." The witnesses note Joseph's Davidic descent and the fulfilment of prophecy. Gill notes Joseph lived in Galilee, working as a carpenter, having removed from his native place. Barnes: "In taking a Jewish census, families were kept distinct; hence all went to the place where their family had resided. Joseph was of the family of David, and hence he went up to the city of David. It is not improbable that he might also have had a small paternal estate in Bethlehem."

The Davidic lineage the witnesses discuss. Lightfoot notes three families of David's line (Joseph's, Mary's, and Hillel's), and the conjecture that Quirinius might have summoned David's stock together to judge "whether some danger might not arise from thence" (the Messiah being expected from David). Ellicott: "It had never ceased to glory in the fact that it had been David's city," and what on one view "was the special prerogative of Joseph was that the two lines of natural descent... met in him" — possibly the meaning of the two words "house" and "lineage."

Cyril of Jerusalem (the lone father here) defends the virgin birth against the Joseph-father heresy: those who "say that He was the child of Joseph and Mary, because it is written, And he took unto him his wife," err; for "as Rachel before the wedded state, merely because there was a promise, was called the wife of Jacob, so also Mary, because she had been betrothed, was called the wife of Joseph."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Joseph, of the house and lineage of David, went from Nazareth to Bethlehem, the city of David, in obedience to the census — the very means by which the Son of David would be born in David's city (Micah 5:2). The Davidic descent (of both Joseph and, the witnesses hold, Mary, since the throne-promise required it) is the thread the verse marks; the census, ordering David's stock to their ancestral town, fulfilled the prophecy. Bethlehem, which "had never ceased to glory" in being David's city, now received its greatest Son. The verse anchors the genealogical claim — Jesus the Son of David by legal descent through Joseph and (the witnesses argue) by blood through Mary — in the historical fact of the enrolment. The carpenter of Nazareth, royal in lineage though poor in estate, brings the King to be born where the prophets foretold.
5:
"To be taxed with Mary his espoused wife, being great with child." The witnesses note Mary's accompanying Joseph and her own Davidic connection. Gill: "she came along with him to be taxed and enrolled also, because she was of the same family of David, and belonged to the same city." Clarke notes Mary need not have gone for the census itself — "Joseph's presence could have answered the end proposed" — "but God so ordered it, that the prophecy of Micah should be thus fulfilled, and that Jesus should be born in the city of David (Micah 5:2)."

The grammatical voices weigh the word "espoused." Robertson: "Same verb as in 1:27, but here it really means married or espoused as Matthew 1:24f shows. Otherwise she could not have travelled with Joseph." He argues Mary too was of David's line: "If so, that means that Mary's family register was in Bethlehem also and that she also belonged to the house of David... The Syriac Sinaitic expressly says that both Joseph and Mary were of the house and city of David." Ellicott notes some MSS omit "wife": "with Mary who was betrothed to him," and that "her state, as being great with child, made her, in a special sense, dependent on Joseph's presence and protection."

The phrase "great with child" the witnesses note as setting the scene for the birth. Mary, near her time, undertook the journey — whether required to register or simply needing Joseph's protection — and so was at Bethlehem when the days were fulfilled (v.6). The hardship of the journey at such a time the witnesses (with Calvin, under v.7) read as part of the lowliness into which the Son of God was born.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Mary, espoused (truly married, Robertson) to Joseph and "great with child," accompanied him to Bethlehem — whether to be registered as herself of David's line, or for the protection her condition required. Either way, God so ordered it that she was at Bethlehem when her time came, fulfilling Micah's prophecy. The verse marks Mary's Davidic connection (which the throne-promise of 1:32 and Luke's genealogy require) and the providence that brought her, near her delivery, to the appointed place of birth. The hardship of the journey at such a season belongs to the lowliness of the incarnation. The espoused virgin, heavy with the world's Redeemer, travels the rough road to David's city, that the Scripture might be fulfilled and the King be born where the prophets foretold.
6:
"And so it was, that, while they were there, the days were accomplished that she should be delivered." The witnesses note the time and the manner of the birth. The fathers dwell on the virgin birth. Ambrose: "St. Luke has briefly explained the manner, time, and place... the manner, that is, in which the espoused has conceived, a virgin has born offspring." Gregory of Nyssa: "while His being born of a woman tells of human nature, virginity becoming capable of childbirth betokens something above man... His mother's burden was light, the birth immaculate, the delivery without pain... For as she who by her guilt engrafted death into our nature was condemned to bring forth in trouble, it was meet that she who brought life into the world should accomplish her delivery with joy."

The fathers read the Bethlehem setting richly. Bede: Christ was born there "as a mark of the royal crown" and "on account of the sacrament of the name." Gregory the Great: "Bethlehem is by interpretation the house of bread. For it is the Lord Himself who says, I am the bread of life which came down from heaven." Bede applies it spiritually: "down to the very end of time, the Lord ceases not to be conceived at Nazareth, to be born at Bethlehem, whenever any of His hearers... makes himself a house of eternal bread... in the mind of believers, Christ is conceived by faith, born by baptism."

The grammatical voices note the providence of the timing. Robertson parses "that she should be delivered." Wesley: "Mary seems not to have known that the child must have been born in Bethlehem, agreeably to the prophecy. But the providence of God took care for it." The days were fulfilled while they were at Bethlehem — neither too early at Nazareth nor on the road, but in the appointed city.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: while Joseph and Mary were at Bethlehem, the days of her delivery were accomplished — the providence of God timing the birth to the appointed place, that Micah's prophecy might be fulfilled. The fathers read the virgin birth as above nature: the delivery painless and immaculate, joy replacing the sorrow of Eve's curse; and Bethlehem ("house of bread") as the fitting birthplace of the Bread of Life. The verse marks the fulfilment of the time in the fulfilment of the place — God ordering both the season and the city of the Saviour's birth. Mary, perhaps unaware that the prophecy required Bethlehem, was brought there by the census in time for her delivery; and the Bread of Life was born in the house of bread, to feed the souls of the elect with the bread of his flesh.
7:
"And she brought forth her firstborn son, and wrapped him in swaddling clothes, and laid him in a manger; because there was no room for them in the inn." The witnesses dwell on the poverty and humility of the birth, and debate the word "firstborn." The fathers read the manger and swaddling clothes as the chosen lowliness of God. Chrysostom: "if He had so willed it, He might have come moving the heavens... but such was not the way of His going forth; His desire was not to destroy, but to save; and to trample upon human pride from its very birth, therefore He is not only man, but a poor man." Bede: "He who clothes the whole world... is wrapped up in common linen, that we might receive the best robe... He is confined in the narrow space of a rude manger, whose seat is the heavens, that He may give us ample room in the joys of His heavenly kingdom." Ambrose: "On your account then am I weak, in you am I strong. On your account am I poor, in you am I rich."

On "firstborn," a genuine debate arises over Mary's later children. Jerome answers Helvidius: "Every only-begotten is firstborn, not every firstborn is only-begotten. We say not that he is first-begotten whom others follow, but before whom there is no one." Robertson takes the contrary: "The expression naturally means that she afterwards had other children and we read of brothers and sisters of Jesus. There is not a particle of evidence for the notion that Mary refused to bear other children." Ellicott and Barnes note the question turns on the natural sense of the word and the references to Jesus' brethren.

The grammatical voices explain the setting. Robertson: the "manger" is "a crib in a stall whether in a cave (Justin Martyr) or connected with the inn"; the "inn" (kataluma) "a lodging-house or khan, poor enough at best, but there was not even room... because of the crowds for the census." Clarke argues phatne means "the whole stable." Barnes and Ellicott describe the Eastern khan and the cave-tradition of the Nativity. They note the birth showed poverty but also that "Jesus was born poor... it is no dishonour to be poor" (Barnes).

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine of the self-emptying: "We see here not only the great poverty of Joseph, but the cruel tyranny... When he was thrown into a stable, and placed in a manger, and a lodging refused him among men, it was that heaven might be opened to us, not as a temporary lodging, but as our eternal country" — for "he had taken upon him our flesh for this purpose, that he might empty himself (Philippians 2:7) on our account." The witnesses agree: the Son of God was born in poverty and laid in a manger, there being no room in the inn — the chosen lowliness by which he emptied himself for us, that we might be made rich (the fathers' refrain). On "firstborn" they divide: a title implying no other children (Jerome) or naturally implying them (Robertson). The verse displays the humility of the incarnation: the King of glory in a manger, the Bread of Life in the house of bread, refused a lodging among men that he might open heaven to us.
8:
"And there were in the same country shepherds abiding in the field, keeping watch over their flock by night." The witnesses note the lowly recipients of the first announcement and read the shepherds spiritually. The fathers mark the choice of humble men. Chrysostom: "to the shepherds in visible shape as to men of a ruder nature. But the Angel went not to Jerusalem, sought not for Scribes and Pharisees (for they were corrupt and tormented with envy). But these were simple men living in the ancient practices of Moses and the Patriarchs. There is a certain road which leads by innocence to Philosophy." Bede notes the night: "No where in the Old Testament do we find that the Angels who appear to the Patriarchs came in the day time. This privilege was rightly kept for this time, when there arose in the darkness a light to them that were true of heart."

The fathers read the shepherds mystically. Gregory: "they are thought worthy above the rest to see sublime things who take a watchful care of their faithful flocks." Bede: "those shepherds, and their flocks, signify all teachers and guides of faithful souls. The night in which they were keeping watch... indicates the dangerous temptations from which they never cease to keep themselves." Origen rises higher: "there were certain shepherd angels who direct the affairs of men, and while each one was keeping his watch, an angel came at the birth of the Lord, and announced that the true Shepherd had arisen."

The puritan Henry draws the contrast with the Magi: "We had in Matthew an account of the notice given to the wise men, who were Gentiles, by a star; here we are told of the notice given to the shepherds, who were Jews, by an angel: to each God chose to speak in the language they were most conversant with." And the honour to lowly callings: "The angel was not sent to the chief priests or the elders... but to a company of poor shepherds, who were like Jacob, plain men dwelling in tents... The patriarchs were shepherds. Moses and David particularly were called from keeping sheep."

The grammatical voices (Gill, Lightfoot) note Bethlehem's pastures (David kept sheep there) and the season of field-watching. The witnesses agree: the first announcement of the Saviour's birth came not to the great and learned at Jerusalem but to poor shepherds watching by night near Bethlehem — humble, simple men, like the patriarchs, Moses, and David before them. God chose the lowly to receive the glad tidings, and chose the night, when "there arose in the darkness a light to them that were true of heart" (Bede). The fathers read the shepherds as figures of the Church's teachers, vigilant over their flocks; and the Good Shepherd was born to recall his scattered sheep. The verse displays the grace that passes by the proud and reveals itself to the humble — the first heralds of the gospel being shepherds, fit witnesses to the Shepherd of souls.
9:
"And, lo, the angel of the Lord came upon them, and the glory of the Lord shone round about them: and they were sore afraid." The witnesses note the angelic appearance, the glory, and the shepherds' fear. On "the glory of the Lord," the grammatical voices invoke the Shekinah. Ellicott: "The word suggests the thought of the Shechinah, or cloud of intolerable brightness, which was the token of the divine presence in the Tabernacle and the Temple (1 Kings 8:10-11; Isaiah 6:1-3)... What had been the privilege of patriarchs and priests was now granted to shepherds." Barnes: "an exceedingly great or bright luminous appearance — perhaps not unlike what Paul saw on the way to Damascus." Robertson notes the angel "stepped by their side," and they "feared a great fear."

Clarke pictures the scene: "the angel appeared in the air at some little distance above them, and that from him the rays of the glory of the Lord shone round about them, as the rays of light are projected from the sun. They were sore afraid — Terrified... probably fearing that he was a messenger of justice."

The Reformed Calvin explains the purpose of the glory: "it was in order that they might know that it was the angel of the Lord that spoke. For it would have been of little avail to be told by an angel what is related by Luke, if God had not testified, by some outward sign, that what they heard proceeded from Him. The angel appeared, not in an ordinary form, or without majesty, but surrounded with the brightness of heavenly glory, to affect powerfully the minds of the shepherds, that they might receive the discourse... as coming from the mouth of God himself." The fear, he adds (as elsewhere), is how "God usually humbles the hearts of men, and disposes them to receive his word with reverence."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel of the Lord appeared to the shepherds, surrounded by the glory of the Lord — the Shekinah, the cloud of divine brightness once granted to patriarchs and priests, now to lowly shepherds — and they were filled with great fear. Calvin notes the purpose of the glory: an outward sign by which God testified that the message proceeded from him, that the shepherds might receive it as from God's own mouth. The fear was the holy awe by which God humbles men to receive his word with reverence — soon to be turned to joy by the angel's "Fear not" (v.10). The verse displays the breaking-in of heaven's glory upon the humble: the privilege of the temple's Shekinah granted to men watching sheep, that they might be sure the glad tidings came from God. The brightness that filled the tabernacle now shone on a field, announcing the Word made flesh.
10:
"And the angel said unto them, Fear not: for, behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy, which shall be to all people." The witnesses note the angel's reassurance and the universal scope of the joy. Ellicott notes "Fear not" is "almost the normal accompaniment of the angelic manifestations... intended to lessen, not to increase the dread." On "good tidings," Robertson: "I evangelize to you a great joy" — the verb from which "gospel" comes. On "all people," Ellicott reads it first of Israel: "Better, to all the people. The words point, in the first instance, to the joy which shall be for Israel as God's people."

The Reformed Calvin treats both the joy and its extent at length. On the joy: "until men have peace with God, and are reconciled to him through the grace of Christ, all the joy that they experience is deceitful... The commencement of solid joy is, to perceive the fatherly love of God toward us... Let us learn to be so delighted with Christ alone, that the perception of his grace may overcome, and at length remove from us, all the distresses of the flesh." On the extent: "this joy was common to all people, because it was indiscriminately offered to all... When the angel says that this joy shall be to all the people, he speaks of the chosen people only; but now that the middle wall of partition (Ephesians 2:14) has been thrown down, the same message has reference to the whole human race." Clarke makes the same point against a variant: some MSS wrongly read "to us" (including angels), "but St. Paul says, he took not upon him the nature of angels... therefore the good news is to you... to all people."

The angel's "behold" the witnesses note as fixing attention on the great announcement. The shepherds' fear (v.9) is met with the assurance of glad tidings — not judgment but joy, not for them only but for all the people, and (with the wall broken down) for all mankind.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel turns the shepherds' fear to joy, announcing good tidings — the very word from which "gospel" is formed — of great joy for all the people. Calvin draws the substance: solid joy begins in peace with God through Christ; and the joy, first offered to Israel ("the people"), now reaches the whole human race, the middle wall of partition (Ephesians 2:14) being thrown down. The verse proclaims the gospel in miniature: glad tidings, great joy, universal scope. The "Fear not" of the angel opens the way for the message of salvation, which is no narrow blessing but a joy offered indiscriminately to all — though, Calvin adds, enjoyed only by those who believe. The herald of heaven brings to trembling shepherds the first gospel sermon: joy for all people in the Saviour born this day.
11:
"For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Saviour, which is Christ the Lord." The witnesses unfold the three great titles and the emphatic "unto you." On the titles, the Greek Exposition (under v.10 area): "the Savior has reference to the action, the Lord to the dignity of the person... Christ has reference to the adoration, and signifies the compound substance of two natures." Clarke expounds each: "Saviour" (sozein, to make safe); "Christ" (the anointed — prophet, priest, and king, "none but Jesus the Christ" held all three offices); "the Lord" (Kurios, the supreme eternal Being, translating Jehovah). Robertson notes "Christ the Lord" (Christos Kurios) "occurs nowhere else in the N.T."; it may mean "Messiah, Lord" or "Anointed Lord."

The Reformed Calvin stresses the personal application in "unto you." The angel recalls the Law and the Prophets: "the Redeemer long ago promised... He opens his embassy by referring to the Law and the Prophets." And on the pronoun: "it is not without reason that this word is added... For it would hardly be of service to know that the Savior is born, unless each applied that to his own person, being persuaded that it is for him that the Son of God is born. In the same manner Isaiah says, Unto us a child is born, unto us a Son is given (Isaiah 9:6)." On "Saviour" he pleads the richness of the Greek soter, for which Latin has no full equivalent: "Christ is called Savior, because he bestows a complete salvation."

The grammatical voices note the title's currency. Robertson: "The people under Rome's rule came to call the emperor Saviour and Christians took the word and used it of Christ." Gill stresses "unto you... not unto angels," for whom no Saviour was provided.

The witnesses agree: the angel announces the Saviour, Christ the Lord, born this day in David's city — three titles naming his work (Saviour), his anointing (Christ, prophet-priest-king), and his dignity (the Lord, Jehovah). Calvin draws the heart of the verse from the pronoun "unto you": the gospel profits us only when each applies the Saviour to himself, persuaded that for him the Son of God is born (Isaiah 9:6). The verse is the Christmas gospel in its fullest form: not merely that a Saviour is born, but that he is born for you — Christ, the anointed of the three offices, and the Lord, the eternal God. The emperor's borrowed title "Saviour" belongs in truth to the child of Bethlehem; and the certainty of faith is to make the "unto you" one's own.
12:
"And this shall be a sign unto you; Ye shall find the babe wrapped in swaddling clothes, lying in a manger." The witnesses note the lowly sign and its meaning. Ellicott: "The sign was not such in itself, but became so by its agreement with the prediction. It was something exceptional that a new-born infant should be found, not in a cradle, but in a manger; still stranger that that infant babe should be the heir of the House of David." Barnes: "The evidence by which you shall know the child is that you will find him wrapped in swaddling clothes and lying in a manger."

Clarke draws the lesson of humility: "You shall find this glorious person, however strange it may appear, wrapped in swaddling clothes, lying in a stable! It is by humility that Christ comes to reign; and this is the only way into his kingdom! Pride is the character of all the children of Adam: humility the mark of the Son of God."

The Reformed Calvin reads the sign as God's way of meeting the prejudice of the lowly appearance: "The angel meets the prejudice which might naturally hinder the faith of the shepherds; for what a mockery is it, that he, whom God has sent to be the King, and the only Savior, is seen lying in a manger! That the mean and despicable condition... might not deter the shepherds, the angel tells them beforehand what they would see." He compares it to the sacraments: "This method, which might appear absurd and almost ridiculous, the Lord pursues toward us every day. Sending down to us from heaven the word of the Gospel, he enjoins us to embrace Christ crucified, and holds out to us in earthly and fading elements... He places before our eyes a little water; by a small portion of bread and wine, he seals the promise of eternal life." And he draws the application: "no sign, however mean in itself, ought to hide his glory from our view."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sign given to the shepherds was the babe in swaddling clothes lying in a manger — not a sign in itself, but a sign by its agreement with the angel's word (Ellicott), and a lesson that Christ comes to reign by humility (Clarke). Calvin reads it deeply: the angel forewarns the shepherds of the mean appearance, lest the manger deter their faith — just as God daily holds out Christ to us in the lowly elements of word and sacrament, that no meanness of sign should hide his glory. The verse teaches that faith must look past the lowly outward to the glory testified by God's word: the King of glory in a manger, recognized not by his cradle but by the heavenly announcement. The humblest of signs points to the highest of persons; and the shepherds, taking God at his word, find the Saviour where the angel said.
13:
"And suddenly there was with the angel a multitude of the heavenly host praising God, and saying." The witnesses note the angelic army and their praise. The fathers read the host and their song. Bede: "Lest the authority of a single Angel should appear small... straightway there was present a multitude of the heavenly host. Rightly has the attending Chorus of Angels received the name of heavenly host, seeing they both humbly bring their aid to that Leader mighty in battle... and also by their celestial arms bravely vanquish those opposing powers." Chrysostom marks the change from judgment to praise: "Of old, Angels were sent to punish... Now on the other hand they sing the song of thanksgiving to God, because He has revealed to them His coming down to men." Gregory: "they rejoice also that their number is completed" by our redemption.

The grammatical voices explain "heavenly host." Ellicott: "the host of heaven is common in the later books of the Old Testament, but is there used as including the visible hosts of sun, moon, and stars... Here it is obviously used of the angels of God as forming the armies of the great King." Robertson: "A military term for a band of soldiers... Bengel says: Here the army announces peace." Barnes and Clarke note the angels came to honour the newborn Prince of Peace and to teach the shepherds what to think of him.

The Reformed Calvin reads the host as a confirmation of faith: "God determined to adorn his own Son in a still more illustrious manner... here is a heavenly host, with one consent and one voice bearing testimony to the Son of God. What then would be our obstinacy, if we refused to join with the choir of angels?... how abominable in the sight of God must unbelief be, which disturbs this delightful harmony between heaven and earth." He draws the lesson of unity: "the Lord intended to recommend to us the unity of faith, and to exhort us to join with one consent in singing his praises on earth."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which introduces the Gloria. The witnesses agree: suddenly a multitude of the heavenly host — the army of the great King — joined the single angel, praising God. The fathers read the host as the army that vanquishes the powers of the air and rejoices that our redemption completes their number; and they mark the change from the angels of judgment of old to the angels of thanksgiving now, at God's coming down to men. Calvin draws the confirmation of faith: the whole heavenly host bearing witness to the Son of God, that our unbelief, refusing to join their choir, might be shown abominable, and that we might join with one consent in singing his praises on earth. The verse opens the angelic anthem (v.14): heaven's army turned choir, announcing peace and praising God for the Saviour born.
14:
"Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men." The witnesses expound the angelic anthem and debate its text. On the meaning, Barnes: "the praise of redeeming man is due to God... Nowhere could his glory be more strikingly exhibited than in giving his only-begotten Son to die for men." On "in the highest," he notes it may mean the highest strains, or among the highest (the angels), or in the highest heavens. On "peace," he lists the gospel's peace: reconciling the world to God, bringing the sinner to peace with his Maker, diffusing good-will among men and nations.

A genuine textual debate concerns the last clause. The Authorized reading takes "good will toward men" (eudokia, nominative) as a third member. But the critical text reads the genitive (eudokias): "peace among men of good will / men with whom God is well pleased." Robertson: "the genitive eudokias is undoubtedly correct, supported by the oldest and best uncials... Glory and Peace correspond, in the highest and on earth, to God and among men of goodwill... real peace on earth exists only among those who are the subjects of God's goodwill." Ellicott agrees: "among men who are the objects of the good will, the approval and love of God." Calvin too rejects the Vulgate genitive only to land on the divine good-pleasure as the source: "the angels speak of it as the source of peace, and thus inform us that peace is a free gift, and flows from the pure mercy of God."

On "peace," Calvin guards against misreading it as outward concord: "they do not speak of an outward peace cultivated by men with each other; but they say that the earth is at peace, when men have been reconciled to God... A short and clear definition of peace may be obtained from two opposite things — the wrath of God and the dread of death." Ellicott notes the connection (made by Milton's Ode) with the Pax Romana, but insists "the peace of the angels' hymn is something far higher... peace between man and God." Lightfoot reads "good will toward men" as the subject: "The good will of God towards men is glory to God in the highest, and peace on earth."

The witnesses agree on the substance — the anthem ascribes glory to God for the redemption now begun, and announces peace on earth — while dividing on the text of the last clause: the Authorized "good will toward men" (a third member) or the critical "peace among men of God's good pleasure." Either way, the peace is no mere outward concord but reconciliation with God, flowing from his free good-will and mercy. The verse is the song of the incarnation: heaven glorified, earth at peace with God, the divine good-will revealed in the gift of the Son — the highest praise above answered by the deepest peace below.
15:
"And it came to pass, as the angels were gone away from them into heaven, the shepherds said one to another, Let us now go even unto Bethlehem, and see this thing which is come to pass, which the Lord hath made known unto us." The witnesses note the shepherds' prompt resolve. The fathers dwell on their faith. Bede: "As men who were truly watching, they said not, Let us see the child, but the word which has come to pass, i.e. the Word which was from the beginning, let us see how it has been made flesh for us." Ambrose: "Let not this seem to you a slight example of faith, because of the humble character of the shepherds. For simplicity is sought for, not pride... no one indolently seeks after Christ." Origen reads the finding: "They found Mary (her who had brought Jesus into the world), and Joseph (the guardian of our Lord's birth), and the babe lying in the manger (the Savior Himself)."

The grammatical voices note the Hebraism. Ellicott: "Literally, this word, or spoken thing. The choice of the Greek word seems to indicate that St. Luke was translating from the Aramaic." Robertson: "A Hebraistic and vernacular use of rhema (something said) as something done."

The Reformed Calvin reads the shepherds' obedience as a model. They were not expressly commanded to go, "but, being sufficiently aware that such was the design of God, they hasten to see Christ... our delay in coming admits of no excuse." He draws the lesson of the word taking root: "Instead of allowing the word of God, as many do, to pass away with the sound, we must take care that it strike its roots deep in us, and manifest its power, as soon as the sound has died away upon our ears." And of mutual exhortation: "it is not enough that each of us is attentive to his own duty, if we do not give mutual exhortations." They rightly say the thing was "made known to them," for "they consider the messenger of God to possess the same authority as if the Lord himself had addressed them."

The witnesses agree: as the angels departed, the shepherds resolved at once to go to Bethlehem to see what the Lord had made known — a model of faith's prompt obedience. The fathers note they sought to see "the Word made flesh," not merely a child, and that none seeks Christ indolently. Calvin draws the lessons: the word must strike root and bear fruit as soon as the sound dies away; believers must exhort one another; and the message of God's messenger carries God's own authority. The verse displays the obedience of faith: the shepherds, taking the angelic word as God's own, hasten without delay to find the Saviour. They go not to gaze at a spectacle but to see how the eternal Word has been made flesh for them — and their eager haste rebukes our slowness to come to Christ.
16:
"And they came with haste, and found Mary, and Joseph, and the babe lying in a manger." The witnesses note the shepherds' eager search and finding. Gill: "They came with haste, in the night, leaving their flocks... The wonderfulness of the vision, the importance of the thing related, the eagerness of their spirits to see the thing that was told them, put them on making quick dispatch." Robertson: "Found (aneuran). The compound ana suggests a search before finding." Barnes draws the application: "These shepherds appear to have been pious men... On the first intimation that he had actually appeared they went with haste to find him. So all men should without delay seek the Saviour... We may always find him. We need not travel to Bethlehem. We have only to cast our eyes to heaven."

The Reformed Calvin reads the finding as faith rising above the mean appearance: "This was a revolting sight, and was sufficient of itself to produce an aversion to Christ. For what could be more improbable than to believe that he was the King of the whole people, who was deemed unworthy to be ranked with the lowest of the multitude?... Yet Luke writes, that none of these things prevented the shepherds from admiring and praising God. The glory of God was so fully before their eyes, and reverence for his Word was so deeply impressed upon their minds, that the elevation of their faith easily rose above all that appeared mean or despicable in Christ." He draws the lesson: "the only reason why our faith is either retarded or driven from the proper course, by some very trifling obstacles, is, that we do not look steadfastly enough on God."

The finding of "Mary, and Joseph, and the babe" the witnesses note as the confirmation of the sign. The shepherds found all exactly as the angel had said (v.12) — the babe in the manger — and so their faith was rewarded and confirmed by sight.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the shepherds came with haste — leaving their flocks by night, eager and searching — and found Mary, Joseph, and the babe in the manger, exactly as the angel had foretold. Calvin draws the heart: the mean sight (a King in a manger) might have produced aversion, yet the shepherds' faith, fixed on God's glory and reverencing his word, rose above all that appeared despicable in Christ — a rebuke to our faith, hindered by trifling obstacles because we do not look steadfastly on God. Barnes applies it to all: as the shepherds sought the Saviour without delay, so should we — and we need not travel to Bethlehem, for we may always find him by faith. The verse displays faith rewarded: the eager search confirmed by the sign, the lowly manger no barrier to those who reverence God's word. The shepherds found the Saviour where the angel said, and their faith was crowned with sight.
17:
"And when they had seen it, they made known abroad the saying which was told them concerning this child." The witnesses note the shepherds' testimony. Gill: having seen the child, they published "this whole affair, as had been related to them." The shepherds became the first heralds of the gospel, spreading the word the angel had told them.

The Reformed Calvin and the grammatical voices read the shepherds' proclamation as the fruit of faith. Having received the angelic word and confirmed it by sight, they could not be silent but made it known to others — a pattern of how those who find Christ become witnesses to him. The shepherds, simple men, became the first evangelists, declaring the Saviour's birth to all who would hear.

The fathers (under v.15) had noted that "to this end have the Shepherds of the Church been ordained, that what they have learned in the Scriptures they might explain to their hearers" (Bede); the literal shepherds figure the spiritual. Having seen, they proclaim; and their testimony, with its "simple eloquence proclaiming the truth" (Gloss), stirs wonder in all who hear (v.18).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: having seen the child, the shepherds made known abroad the saying told them concerning him — becoming the first heralds of the gospel, witnesses to the Saviour's birth. Their testimony is the fruit of faith: having received the angelic word and confirmed it by sight, they could not keep silent but published it to all. The verse displays the pattern of evangelism: those who find Christ become witnesses to him, declaring what they have seen and heard. The simple shepherds, fit figures of the Church's teachers, proclaim with artless truthfulness the great thing God had made known to them; and their word, stirring wonder in all who hear (v.18), is the first spreading abroad of the good tidings of great joy. The lowly first to hear become the first to tell; and the manger's secret, declared by shepherds, begins its journey to all the world. Faith that finds the Saviour cannot but proclaim him.
18:
"And all they that heard it wondered at those things which were told them by the shepherds." The briefest verse — with Gill — records the wonder of the hearers. Gill notes they marvelled at "what the shepherds related of what they had heard from the angel, and from Joseph and Mary, and what they had seen themselves." The testimony of the shepherds stirred amazement in all who heard.

The wonder the witnesses (with the Gloss, under v.15) read as the natural response to the marvellous tidings. The Gloss: "Not only do they marvel at the mystery of the incarnation, but also at so wonderful an attestation of the shepherds, men who could not have devised these unheard of things, but were with simple eloquence proclaiming the truth." The artless testimony of humble men, who could not have invented such things, carried its own weight; and the hearers wondered both at the mystery proclaimed and at the manner of its proclamation.

The wonder, however, the witnesses note is not yet faith. To marvel at the report is one thing; to believe and ponder it (as Mary did, v.19) is another. The hearers wondered; Mary kept and weighed. The verse marks the first ripple of the gospel's spreading — astonishment at the news — which in some would deepen to faith and in others fade.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: all who heard the shepherds' report wondered at the things told them — the natural response to the marvellous tidings of the incarnation, attested by humble men who could not have devised them. The wonder, the Gloss notes, was both at the mystery proclaimed and at the simple, truthful eloquence of the shepherds. Yet wonder is not yet faith: to marvel at the report is one thing, to believe and ponder it (as Mary did, v.19) is another. The verse marks the first spreading of the good tidings beyond the manger — astonishment at the news of a Saviour born — the seed of the gospel sown in many hearts, to deepen in some to faith and to fade in others. The shepherds' testimony, artless and true, set the whole company wondering; and the report of the Saviour's birth began its journey through the world, stirring amazement wherever it was told. The lowly heralds had done their work, and the wonder they kindled would, in due time, ripen into the praise of God.
19:
"But Mary kept all these things, and pondered them in her heart." The witnesses note Mary's meditative faith, and several mark her as the source of the narrative. Barnes: "Here is a delicate and beautiful expression of the feelings of a mother... Everything they do or suffer... is treasured up in her mind... She kept them; she revolved them; she weighed them in her mind, giving to each circumstance its just importance." Clarke: "Weighing them in her heart... Every circumstance relative to her son's birth, Mary treasured up in her memory; and every new circumstance she weighed, or compared with those which had already taken place."

Ellicott and Robertson note that the intimate touch points to Mary as Luke's source. Ellicott: "On the assumption that the whole narrative is traceable to the Virgin herself as its first author, these brief and simple touches as to her own feelings are of singular interest. She could not as yet understand all that had been said and done, but she received it in faith." Robertson: "She kept on keeping together (syn-) all these things. They were meat and drink to her... But did not Mary keep also a Baby Book? And may not Luke have seen it?... Mary would go over each detail... and brood over it all with a mother's high hopes and joy."

The Reformed Calvin draws the example for all believers: "Mary's diligence in contemplating the works of God is laid before us for two reasons; first, to inform us, that this treasure was laid up in her heart, for the purpose of being published to others at the proper time; and, secondly, to afford to all the godly an example for imitation. For, if we are wise, it will be the chief employment, and the great object of our life, to consider with attention those works of God which build up our faith." On "pondered" (symballein): "to collect the several events which agreed in proving the glory of Christ, so that they might form one body."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: in contrast to those who merely wondered (v.18), Mary kept all these things and pondered them — weighing and comparing each circumstance in her heart, treasuring them in faith though she could not yet understand all. Several witnesses see in this intimate touch the mark of Luke's source: the narrative traceable to the Virgin herself, who laid up these things to be published at the proper time. Calvin draws the example for all the godly: to make the contemplation of God's faith-building works the chief employment of life. The verse displays meditative faith — the difference between wonder and ponder. Mary did not merely marvel at the report; she gathered the events, compared prophecy with fulfilment, and brooded over them with a mother's hope, so that all the witnesses to Christ's glory "might form one body." The Virgin's pondering heart is both the well from which the gospel narrative was drawn and the pattern of faithful meditation on the works of God.
20:
"And the shepherds returned, glorifying and praising God for all the things that they had heard and seen, as it was told unto them." The witnesses note the shepherds' return in praise. Barnes: "To their flocks. Glorifying — giving honour to God, and celebrating his praises." Clarke draws the application to ministers: "These simple men, having satisfactory evidence of the truth of the good tidings, and feeling a Divine influence upon their own minds, returned to the care of their flocks, glorifying God... After having compared the predictions of the prophets with the facts... they will return to their flocks, glorifying and praising God for what they had seen and heard."

The Reformed Calvin reads the shepherds' praise as a rebuke to our coldness: "The shepherds knew with certainty that this was a work of God. Their zeal in glorifying God is an implied reproof of our indolence, or rather of our ingratitude. If the cradle of Christ had such an effect upon them, as to make them rise from the stable and the manger to heaven, how much more powerful ought the death and resurrection of Christ to be in raising us to God?" And he notes the rule of their faith: "When Luke says that the testimony of the angel served as a rule to the shepherds in all that they did, he points out the nature of true godliness. For our faith is properly aided by the works of God, when it directs everything to this end, that the truth of God, which was revealed in his word, may be brought out with greater clearness." Athanasius (under v.15): "every one rejoiced in the nativity of Christ, not with human feelings... but at the presence of Christ and the luster of the Divine light."

The shepherds' return to their flocks the witnesses note as the resumption of ordinary duty transformed by praise. They did not abandon their calling but went back to it glorifying God — the encounter with Christ sanctifying their common work.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the shepherds' narrative. The witnesses agree: the shepherds returned to their flocks glorifying and praising God for all they had heard and seen, finding everything as the angel had told them. Calvin draws the rebuke: their zeal, rising from the manger to heaven, condemns our ingratitude — for if Christ's cradle so moved them, how much more should his death and resurrection move us? And he marks the rule of true godliness: the angelic word served as the standard by which they measured all they saw, that God's revealed truth might shine more clearly. The verse displays faith's proper end — praise — and the sanctifying of ordinary duty: the shepherds went back to their common calling, but transformed, glorifying God. Having found the Saviour, they returned to their flocks with songs; and their grateful praise, kindled by the manger, is the pattern of every soul that has met Christ and goes back to its daily work rejoicing.
21:
"And when eight days were accomplished for the circumcising of the child, his name was called JESUS, which was so named of the angel before he was conceived in the womb." The witnesses note Christ's circumcision and naming, and read the reasons he submitted to the rite. The fathers give several reasons. Epiphanius (against the Ebionites who urged it as binding): Christ was circumcised "to show the reality of His flesh, in opposition to Manichaeus... that He might prove that His body was not of the same substance with the Deity... to add a confirmation to circumcision which He had instituted to wait His coming... to leave no excuse to the Jews." Bede: "He was circumcised that He might enjoin upon us by His example the virtue of obedience, and might take compassion on them who being placed under the law were unable to bear the burdens of the law." Athanasius and Cyril read it as a figure of baptism: "circumcision expressed nothing else but the stripping off of the old birth... done as a sign of the future baptism through Christ. Therefore as soon as that of which it was a sign came, the figure ceased."

The puritan Henry expounds Christ "made under the law (Galatians 4:4)": "Though it was a painful operation... yet Christ would undergo it for us; nay, therefore he submitted to it, to give an instance of his early obedience, his obedience unto blood. Then he shed his blood by drops, which afterwards he poured out in purple streams." Christ was circumcised "that he might own himself of the seed of Abraham," "a surety for our sins," and to "put an honour upon the dedication of the infant seed of the church to God" — Henry arguing this favours infant baptism more than Christ's baptism at thirty favours deferral.

On the name, the fathers note its heavenly origin. Origen: "the name of Jesus, a glorious name and worthy of all honor... ought not first to be uttered by men, nor by them be brought into the world. Therefore significantly the Evangelist adds, which was called of the Angel." Bede: "Of this name the elect also in their spiritual circumcision rejoice to be partakers, that as from Christ they are called Christians, so from the Savior they may be called saved."

The witnesses agree: on the eighth day Christ was circumcised and named Jesus — submitting, though sinless, to the rite, that he might be "made under the law" (Galatians 4:4) to redeem those under the law. The fathers give the reasons: to prove the reality of his flesh (against docetism), to teach obedience, to fulfil and then end the figure (circumcision giving place to baptism), and to leave the Jews no excuse. The name Jesus, given by the angel before his conception, was of heavenly not human origin, and the elect rejoice to share it in their spiritual circumcision. The verse displays the Son of God beginning his obedience unto blood: circumcised as a surety for our sins, named Saviour by heaven, and submitting to the law that he might redeem us from it.
22:
"And when the days of her purification according to the law of Moses were accomplished, they brought him to Jerusalem, to present him to the Lord." The witnesses note the purification and presentation, and read the original-sin lesson of the law. The fathers note Mary's voluntary submission though exempt. Bede: "the mother of God, as she is free from all connection with man, so is she exempt from any obligation of the law. For not every woman who brings forth, but she who has received seed and brought forth, is pronounced unclean... But that we might be loosed from the bonds of the law, as did Christ, so also Mary submitted herself of her own will to the law." Athanasius reads the presentation for our sake: "as not to confer grace on Himself was He made man and circumcised... so for our sakes is He presented to the Lord, that we also might learn to present ourselves to the Lord." Bede notes the offering: the turtledove (chastity) and pigeon (simplicity) figure the meekness and purity of the saints, and the contemplative and active lives.

The Reformed Calvin makes the purification a proof of original sin: "It is a mistake to imagine that this law of purification was merely political... it placed before the eyes of the Jews both the corruption of their nature, and the remedy of divine grace. This law is of itself abundantly sufficient to prove original sin... there could not be a clearer demonstration of the curse pronounced on mankind than when the Lord declared that the child comes from its mother unclean and polluted... Hence it follows, that all are corrupted in Adam." Yet Christ, "the fountain of all purity, determined to be reckoned unclean... in order to wash away our pollutions" (Galatians 3:13).

The grammatical voices weigh "their purification." Robertson: "The old manuscripts have their instead of her... it is not clear whether their refers to Mary and Joseph... or to Mary and the child." Ellicott reads the law's "primary idea" as "witnessing to the taint of imperfection and sin attaching to every child of man." On the presentation, Ellicott and Robertson note the firstborn son's dedication (Exodus 13:2) and redemption (Numbers 18:15), "a survival of the idea of the priesthood of the firstborn."

The witnesses agree: Joseph and Mary, in devout obedience to the law of Moses, brought Jesus to Jerusalem for Mary's purification and his presentation to the Lord — though both were exempt, Mary by the virgin birth and Christ by his sinlessness, they submitted voluntarily, that they might fulfil the law and we might be loosed from it. Calvin draws the doctrine: the purification law demonstrates original sin (the child born unclean) and its remedy in grace; and Christ, the fountain of purity, took our uncleanness to wash away our pollution. On "their purification" the texts divide (Mary and the child, or Mary and Joseph). The verse displays the holy family's obedience and the law's testimony to human sinfulness — the sinless Christ presented to the Lord, that he might present us.
23:
"(As it is written in the law of the Lord, Every male that openeth the womb shall be called holy to the Lord;)." The witnesses note the law of the firstborn (Exodus 13:2) and its fulfilment in Christ. The fathers (under v.22) read "openeth the womb" of Christ's unique birth. Gregory of Nyssa: "He alone, ineffably conceived and incomprehensibly brought forth, opened the virgin's womb, till then unopened by marriage, and after this birth miraculously retaining the seal of chastity." Ambrose: "no union with man disclosed the secrets of the virgin's womb, but the Holy Spirit infused the immaculate seed into an inviolate womb." So Christ alone is truly "holy" by nature, as Gabriel said (1:35), not merely by dedication.

The grammatical voices note the law and its limits. Robertson: "The law did not require that the child be brought to Jerusalem. The purification concerned the mother, the presentation the son." Paul (Galatians 4:4) "will urge that Jesus was made under the law as Luke here explains." Barnes cites Exodus 13:2, 12.

The Reformed Calvin explains the law's origin and Christ's submission: "The Lord commanded that all the males should be dedicated to him, in remembrance of their deliverance; because when the angel slew all the first-born of Egypt (Exodus 12:29), he had spared the first-born of Israel." And he draws the lesson of obedient worship: "as the Lord is the common Redeemer of all, he has a right to claim us as his own... Luke so frequently repeats the statement, that Joseph and Mary did what was written... For these words teach us, that we must not, at our own suggestion, attempt any thing in the worship of God, but must obediently follow what he requires in his Word."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, a parenthetical citation of the law. The witnesses agree: Luke cites the law of the firstborn (Exodus 13:2) — every male opening the womb is holy to the Lord — instituted in memory of the sparing of Israel's firstborn in Egypt. Christ, "made under the law" (Galatians 4:4), submitted to it; and the fathers note he alone truly "opened the virgin's womb" by a miraculous birth, and alone is "holy" by nature, not merely by dedication. Calvin draws the lesson: God claims his own as Redeemer, and Joseph and Mary's repeated obedience "as it is written" teaches us to worship God only as his Word requires, not by our own invention. The verse anchors the presentation in the law of the firstborn, fulfilled supremely in the firstborn of Mary — the holy one who opened the womb and was presented to the Lord, the redeemer of those whom the law had claimed. The sinless Son submits to the statute of redemption, that he might redeem.
24:
"And to offer a sacrifice according to that which is said in the law of the Lord, A pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons." The witnesses note the offering of the poor and what it reveals of the holy family's poverty. Barnes: those able offered a lamb and a dove; "if not able to bring a lamb, then they were permitted to bring two turtle-doves... By Mary's making this offering she showed her poverty; and our Saviour, by coming in a state of poverty, has shown that it is not dishonourable to be poor... No station is dishonourable where God places us." Clarke: "The rich were required to bring a lamb, but the poor... were required to bring either two turtle doves, or two pigeons. This is a proof that the holy family were not in affluence." Lightfoot explains the rabbinic detail: one bird for a burnt offering, one for a sin offering.

The Reformed Calvin addresses an objection — had not the Magi just given gold? "We must not imagine that they had such abundance of gold as to raise them suddenly from poverty to wealth. We do not read that their camels were laden with gold. It is more probable that it was some small present, which they had brought solely as a mark of respect... There would be no impropriety in saying that Joseph and Mary gave as much as their circumstances allowed, though they reserved a little money to defray the expenses of their journey." He notes the law's mercy: it "drew a line of distinction between [the poor] and the rich, as to the kind of sacrifices, and thus relieved them from burdensome expense."

The fathers (under v.22) read the poverty as Christ's chosen condescension. Bede: "the Lord, though He was rich, deigned to become poor, that by His poverty He might make us partakers of His riches" (2 Corinthians 8:9). Ellicott notes the offering "had, like all other sacrifices, to be made in the Temple," and that this visit "must have preceded the visit of the Magi," after which an immediate flight to Egypt followed.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the holy family offered the sacrifice of the poor — two turtledoves or pigeons, the substitute the law allowed those unable to bring a lamb (Leviticus 12:8) — a clear proof of their poverty. Calvin answers the objection of the Magi's gold: a small gift of respect, not enough to raise them to wealth; they gave as their circumstances allowed. The fathers read the poverty as Christ's chosen condescension — the rich Lord becoming poor that we might be made rich (2 Corinthians 8:9). Barnes draws the dignity of poverty: it is no dishonour, for no station is dishonourable where God places us. The verse displays the lowliness of the incarnation continued: the Saviour's parents, too poor for a lamb, bring the offering of the poor, and the King of glory begins his life among the humble — sanctifying poverty by sharing it.
25:
"And, behold, there was a man in Jerusalem, whose name was Simeon; and the same man was just and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel: and the Holy Ghost was upon him." The witnesses note Simeon's character, his hope, and his Spirit-led reception of Christ. The fathers dwell on his righteousness and expectation. Ambrose: "scarcely is righteousness preserved without fear... that holy fear of the Lord which abides for ever, by which the righteous man, the more ardent his love to God, is so much the more careful not to offend Him." On "waiting for the consolation of Israel," Gregory of Nyssa: "It was not worldly happiness that the prudent Simeon was waiting for as the consolation of Israel, but a real happiness, that is, a passing over to the beauty of truth from the shadow of the law. For he had learnt from the sacred oracles that he would see the Lord's Christ before he should depart."

The fathers read Simeon's taking the child as the law yielding to grace. Bede: "the righteous man, according to the law, received the Child Jesus in his arms, that he might signify that the legal righteousness of works under the figure of the hands and arms was to be changed for the lowly indeed but saving grace of Gospel faith. The old man received the infant Christ, to convey that this world, now worn out with old age, should return to the childlike innocence of the Christian life." Gregory of Nyssa: "blessed those hands which handled the word of life, and the arms which were held out to receive Him!" Origen: "If you will touch Jesus and grasp Him in your hands, strive with all your strength to have the Spirit for your guide, and come to the temple of God."

The puritan Henry and the grammatical voices note Simeon's character and the Spirit upon him. Lightfoot distinguishes this Simeon from the rabbinic Simeon the Just and from Rabban Simeon son of Hillel. Cyril of Alexandria expounds the festival of the Presentation and Christ "made under the law... to redeem them that were under the law."

The witnesses agree: Simeon, a just and devout man of Jerusalem, was waiting for "the consolation of Israel" — not worldly happiness but the Messiah, the passing from the shadow of the law to the truth of the gospel — and the Holy Ghost was upon him, guiding him to the temple to receive the Christ. The fathers read his taking the child in his arms as the law yielding to grace: the old man embracing the infant, the legal righteousness of works giving place to the saving grace of gospel faith, the worn-out world returning to childlike innocence. The verse introduces the aged saint who, led by the Spirit, recognized the long-awaited Redeemer — a fit witness, with the shepherds and the angels, to the birth of the Saviour. Simeon's holy expectation, rewarded at last, is the pattern of every soul that waits in faith for the consolation of God.
26:
"And it was revealed unto him by the Holy Ghost, that he should not see death, before he had seen the Lord's Christ." The witnesses note the divine revelation to Simeon and its gracious promise. On the manner of the revelation, the grammatical voices note its oracular character. Robertson: the Greek verb is "common... to get the advice of the Delphic and other oracles... The LXX and Josephus use it of God's commands"; a papyrus has the noun "for a divine response." Ellicott: "It implies a divine oracular communication... The message in this case came clearly as an answer to prayers and yearnings." Clarke: "He was divinely informed... The secret of the Lord is with them that fear him. The soul of a righteous and devout man is a proper habitation for the Holy Spirit."

On "should not see death," the witnesses note the Hebraism. Barnes: "To see death and to taste of death was a common way among the Hebrews of expressing death itself (Psalm 89:48)." On "the Lord's Christ," Barnes and Ellicott prefer "the Lord's Anointed": "The word retains all the fulness of its meaning — the Messiah, the Anointed of Jehovah" (Ellicott).

The gracious promise the witnesses apply to all believers. Barnes: "To an aged man who had been long waiting for the Messiah, how grateful must have been this revelation... But this revelation is now given to every man, that he need not taste of death till, by the eye of faith, he may see the Christ of God... To us also it will be as great a privilege in our dying hours to have seen Christ by faith as it was to Simeon. It will be the only thing that can support us then." Clarke: "They that seek shall find: it is impossible that a man who is earnestly seeking the salvation of God should be permitted to die without finding it."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Holy Ghost had revealed to Simeon — by a divine oracular communication, answering his prayers and yearnings — that he should not die before seeing the Lord's Christ, the Anointed of Jehovah. Clarke notes the soul of the righteous is a fit habitation for the Spirit, and "the secret of the Lord is with them that fear him." Barnes applies the promise to all: this revelation is now given to every man, that he need not taste death till he has seen Christ by faith — and to have so seen him will be the only support in our dying hours. The verse marks the gracious assurance that crowned Simeon's long expectation: the aged saint, led by the Spirit, would not depart until he had seen the salvation of God. His holy patience, rewarded at the last, is the pledge that those who seek the Lord's Christ shall surely find him — and may, like Simeon, depart in peace.
27:
"And he came by the Spirit into the temple: and when the parents brought in the child Jesus, to do for him after the custom of the law..." The witnesses note the Spirit's leading of Simeon and the providential timing. The Reformed Gill is careful about the manner: "the Spirit of God, who knows and searches all things... knew the exact time when Jesus would be brought into the temple, and suggested to Simeon, and moved upon him, and influenced and directed him, to go thither at that very time" — not, he insists, that Simeon was bodily transported as Ezekiel was. Barnes glosses "into the temple" as "into the court of the women," where the offering for purification was made.

Clarke connects Simeon's coming to prophecy: "probably he had in view the prophecy of Malachi, The Lord, whom ye seek, shall suddenly come to his temple (Malachi 3:1)... now the prophecy was just going to be fulfilled, and the Holy Spirit directed him to go and see its accomplishment." He draws the lesson: "those who come, under the influence of God's Spirit, to places of public worship, will undoubtedly meet with him who is the comfort of Israel."

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the Spirit's secret ordering of the meeting between Simeon and the infant Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that brings the waiting saint and the promised Saviour together at the appointed moment. Simeon came "by the Spirit" — not by chance, nor by mere report (as one corrupt version suggested), but moved and directed by the Holy Ghost, who knew the very hour the child would be brought in. The witnesses note the fitness of the place and the occasion: the temple, where Malachi had foretold the Lord would suddenly come; the court of the women, where Mary fulfilled the law of purification. The same Spirit who had revealed to Simeon that he should not die till he had seen the Lord's Christ now led him to the temple at the precise time of the child's presentation, that the promise might be kept to the letter. Clarke's reflection, the expositors press, turns the scene to abiding use: those who come to worship under the Spirit's influence meet there the Consolation of Israel; and Simeon, faithful in his expectation and obedient to the Spirit's prompting, is rewarded with the sight of the Saviour. The verse thus joins the providence of God and the obedience of his servant, bringing the aged saint to the temple at the very moment the infant Redeemer was carried in.
28:
"Then took he him up in his arms, and blessed God, and said..." The witnesses dwell on the aged saint embracing the infant Saviour. Origen marvels: "if we marvel that a woman was healed by touching the hem of a garment, what must we think of Simeon, who received an Infant in his arms, and rejoiced, seeing that the little one he carried was He who had come to let loose the captive!" — for he knew "no one could release him from the chains of the body with the hope of future life, but He whom he held in his arms." Theophylact draws the confession: "when he says Lord, he confesses that He is the very Lord of both life and death, and so acknowledges the Child whom he held in his arms to be God."

Ambrose makes Simeon a pattern for every believer: "let him come into the temple... let him wait for the Lord's Christ, let him receive in his hands the word of God, and embrace it as it were with the arms of his faith; then let him depart, that he might not see death who has seen life." The Greek expositor notes Simeon "blessed God, because the promises made to him had received their true fulfilment."

So the witnesses make the verse the moment of faith's embrace and the dawning of the Nunc Dimittis. The lesson the expositors draw is the greatness of what Simeon held and the faith with which he held it. The fathers find the scene full of wonder: an old man, near death, takes into his arms a helpless infant — and that infant is the Lord of life and death, the Liberator of the captive, God in the flesh. Simeon's blessing of God, the witnesses note, springs from his recognition: he knows whom he holds, and confesses it in the very title "Lord," owning the child to be God. Ambrose's reading lifts the scene into a model for all the faithful: as Simeon embraced the incarnate Word with the arms of his body, so the believer is to embrace the word of God with the arms of faith; and having so embraced the Christ, he may depart in peace, for he has seen Life itself. The witnesses press the depth of Origen's insight: Simeon felt himself a prisoner of the body, longing for release, and knew that only this child could loose his chains and give him the hope of life beyond death. The verse thus shows faith laying hold of its object — the saint embracing the Saviour — and turning, in that embrace, from longing to fulfilment, from the prison of mortal life to the peace of one who has held the Consolation of Israel in his arms.
29:
"Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace, according to thy word." The witnesses expound the opening of Simeon's song and the peace of his departure. The grammatical-historical Clarke renders it: "Now thou dismissest, loosest him from life, having lived long enough to have the grand end of life accomplished... having seen him, he expects to be immediately dismissed in peace into the eternal world." He notes the delicacy: "though Simeon means his death, yet the thing itself is not mentioned; for death has not only lost its sting, but its name also, to those who have, even by faith, seen the Lord's anointed." Barnes stresses the faithfulness of God: "God never disappoints... to many it might have appeared improbable, when such a promise was made to an old man, that it should be fulfilled; but God fulfils all his word."

Gill notes the humility of "thy servant": Simeon "acknowledges him as his Lord, and to have a despotic power over him with respect to life and death, and himself as his servant." The peace, the witnesses agree, is the tranquillity of one whose deepest desire is satisfied.

So the witnesses make the verse the song of a soul ready to die because it has seen its salvation. The lesson the expositors draw is the peace that the sight of Christ gives in the face of death. Simeon, having received the promise that he should not die till he had seen the Lord's Christ, now holds the fulfilment in his arms, and asks to be dismissed — "loosed" from the body, as a servant from his post — "in peace." The witnesses note the rich humility of his words: he owns God as his sovereign Lord, with power over his life and death, and himself as the servant who waits the Master's leave to depart. And they press the comfort Clarke draws out: to one who has seen Christ, even by faith, death has lost not only its sting but its very name — Simeon speaks not of dying but of being "dismissed in peace," the believer's death being no terror but a gentle release. Barnes's emphasis, the expositors observe, magnifies the faithfulness of God: the promise made to an aged man, seemingly improbable, was kept to the letter, for God never disappoints those who trust him. The verse thus gives voice to the serene readiness of the believing soul that, having beheld its salvation, can lay down its life in peace, assured that the God who kept this promise will keep every other, and that to depart, having seen Christ, is gain.
30:
"For mine eyes have seen thy salvation." The witnesses note that Simeon names the infant Christ "thy salvation." The Reformed Gill expounds the title: the Messiah "goes by the name of salvation, because the salvation of God's elect is put into his hands... because he is the author of it... and because salvation is in him, it is to be had in him." He stresses the double sight: Simeon saw him "with his bodily eyes, a sight which many kings and prophets had desired... and also with the eyes of his understanding, with the spiritual eye of faith, as his Saviour and Redeemer; for without this, the former would not have been sufficient to give such peace."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the Greek "is not the usual feminine noun expressing the abstract idea of salvation, but the neuter of the adjective — that which brings or works out salvation," its use "probably determined by its appearance in the LXX of Isaiah 52:10" (quoted in Luke 3:6). Clarke adds: "Christ is called our salvation, as he is called our life, our peace, our hope — the author of all these to them who believe."

So the witnesses make the verse the ground of Simeon's peace: he has seen, in the child, the salvation of God. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the meaning of the title: Christ himself is "salvation," for in him the whole work of saving the elect is lodged — he is its author, its substance, its dwelling-place, so that to have him is to have salvation. The infant in Simeon's arms is not merely the bringer of salvation but Salvation in person, the One in whom alone deliverance is found.
