Second, the nature of Simeon's sight: he saw Christ not only with bodily eyes, the privilege many prophets longed for and were denied, but with the eye of faith, beholding the child as his own Saviour and Redeemer. The witnesses press, with Gill, that the bodily sight alone would not have given the peace of the Nunc Dimittis; it was the spiritual sight, the faith that laid hold of Christ as personal salvation, that filled Simeon with peace and readiness to die. Ellicott's note on the word — "that which works out salvation," echoing Isaiah's "all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God" (Isaiah 52:10) — the expositors observe, sets Simeon's words in the stream of prophecy: the salvation the prophets foretold, the nations should see, the aged saint now beholds in his arms.
31:
"Which thou hast prepared before the face of all people." The witnesses note the divine preparation and the universal scope of this salvation. The Reformed Gill traces the preparing back through eternity: God prepared Christ as his salvation "in his eternal purposes and decrees... in his counsel and covenant of grace... in the promises and prophecies of the Old Testament, and in all the types, shadows, and sacrifices," and "now had sent him in human nature, to work out that salvation." Clarke pictures it as a feast: "salvation is represented under the notion of a feast, which God himself has provided for the whole world, and to partake of which he has invited all the nations of the earth" — an allusion to Isaiah's "feast of fat things for all people" (Isaiah 25:6).

The grammatical-historical Robertson stresses the universality: "of all the peoples — not merely Jews; another illustration of the universality of Luke's Gospel." Ellicott agrees the word "expresses the universality of the salvation which the next verse contemplates in its application to the two great divisions of the human family."

So the witnesses make the verse the proclamation of a salvation long prepared and meant for all mankind. The lesson the expositors draw is the eternal foresight and the worldwide reach of God's saving work in Christ. The salvation Simeon beheld was no afterthought, the witnesses note, but "prepared" — fixed in God's eternal decree, foreshadowed through all the Old Testament's promises, types, and sacrifices, and now at last sent in the flesh. And it was prepared "before the face of all people" — not for the Jews alone, but, as Luke's Gospel everywhere insists, for all the peoples of the earth. The witnesses press, with Robertson and Ellicott, the distinctive note of Luke's universality: this Gospel, more than the others, presses that the Saviour came for the world, and Simeon's song already sounds the theme that the next verse will divide between Gentile and Jew. Clarke's image of the feast, the expositors observe, captures it well: God has spread, in Christ, a feast of salvation for the whole world, and bids all nations come; salvation is the food of the soul, and he who refuses it must perish. The verse thus lifts the eyes from the infant in the temple to the breadth of God's purpose — a salvation prepared from eternity, displayed before all peoples, and offered to every nation of the earth.
32:
"A light to lighten the Gentiles, and the glory of thy people Israel." The witnesses expound the twofold benefit of the Saviour to the two divisions of mankind. The Reformed Gill expounds "light": "Light is one of the names of the Messiah in the Old Testament" (Isaiah 42:6; 49:6), and Christ is light "as God... as the Creator who lightens every man... and as the Messiah, the light of the Gospel"; "particularly the Gentiles enjoy this benefit of light by him," who before "were in darkness." The grammatical-historical Barnes draws the picture: "the Gentiles are represented as sitting in darkness, in ignorance and sin; Christ is a light to them... as the darkness rolls away when the sun arises, so ignorance and error flee away when Jesus gives light to the mind" (Isaiah 60:3).

On "the glory of thy people Israel," Barnes notes "the first offer of salvation was made to the Jews; Jesus was born among them... hence he was their glory, their honour, their light." The Messiah is at once light to the dark-dwelling Gentiles and the crowning glory of Israel, from whom he sprang.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the Nunc Dimittis, declaring Christ the Saviour of both Gentile and Jew. The lesson the expositors draw is the universal yet ordered scope of redemption. To the Gentiles, who sat in the darkness of ignorance and idolatry, the Christ comes as "a light" — revealing the true God, his law, and the way of salvation, scattering their darkness as the rising sun scatters the night. To Israel, the people of the covenant, he comes as their "glory" — the long-promised Messiah, born among them, in whom their whole history finds its honour and fulfilment. The witnesses note the fitness of the two terms: light, for those who had none; glory, for those entrusted with the oracles and promises, now crowned by the coming of the One they foretold. Simeon's song, the expositors press, thus embraces the whole human family — the nations brought out of darkness into light, and Israel lifted to its true glory in the Saviour it had awaited. And the order, Barnes observes, is the order of the gospel's spread: salvation offered first to the Jew, then to the Gentile, yet meant for all. The aged saint, holding the infant, sees in him the dawn of light for a darkened world and the glory of the people of God — a vision that gathers up the prophets' hope and sends it out to all the peoples of the earth.
33:
"And Joseph and his mother marvelled at those things which were spoken of him." The witnesses note the parents' wonder and a careful question about calling Joseph "father." The Greek expositor explains the marvelling: "the knowledge of supernatural things, as often as it is brought to recollection, renews the miracle in the mind." Origen notes they had now heard such testimony "both by the angel and the multitude of the heavenly host, by the shepherds also, and Simeon." On the title "father" (which the better text gives, where the Received reads "Joseph"), Bede clarifies: "Joseph is called the father of the Saviour, not because he was His real father, but because, from regard to the reputation of Mary, all men considered him so." Augustine deepens it: Joseph "might be called His father in that light in which he is rightly regarded as the husband of Mary — not from any carnal connection, but by reason of the very bond of wedlock, a far closer relationship than that of adoption."

Origen adds the genealogical reason: "since the genealogy is deduced from David to Joseph, lest Joseph should seem to be mentioned for no purpose... he was called His father, that the genealogy might maintain its place." The Greek expositor notes Simeon "blessed them" but gave his presage "only to the mother," lest he deprive Joseph of "the likeness of a father," yet proclaim Mary "the true mother."

So the witnesses make the verse an occasion both for the parents' wonder and for guarding the truth of the virgin birth. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the renewed marvelling of Joseph and Mary: though they had heard much already — from Gabriel, the angelic host, the shepherds — the fresh testimony of Simeon revived their wonder at the mystery of their child, the supernatural truth striking the mind anew with each recollection. Second, and carefully, the sense in which Joseph is called "father." The fathers are unanimous, the witnesses note, that this denotes no carnal paternity: Joseph is Jesus's father by reputation (all men thinking so, for Mary's protection), by the bond of wedlock (Augustine's deeper point, the marriage-tie making him father in a truer sense than adoption), and for the genealogy's sake (Origen, since the line runs through Joseph). The very care with which Simeon blessed both parents yet spoke his prophecy to Mary alone, the expositors observe, preserves the truth: Joseph kept the appearance of fatherhood, but Mary alone was proclaimed the true parent of the child conceived of the Holy Ghost. The verse thus joins the parents' devout astonishment with the Church's careful confession of the virgin birth.
34:
"And Simeon blessed them, and said unto Mary his mother, Behold, this child is set for the fall and rising again of many in Israel; and for a sign which shall be spoken against." The witnesses expound the double effect of Christ and the opposition he would meet. Ambrose notes the grace and the discrimination: "behold what abundant grace is extended to all men by the birth of the Lord, and how prophecy is withheld from the unbelievers, not from the righteous." The grammatical-historical Barnes explains "the fall" as "misery, suffering, disappointment, or ruin," with "plain reference" to the "stone of stumbling and rock of offence" (Isaiah 8:14-15): "many expected a temporal prince, and in this they were disappointed... they rejected him, and fell unto destruction"; while many proud "fell from the height of their own self-righteousness, and were humbled before God, and then rose again to a better righteousness."

The witnesses note the paradox: the same Christ is "set" — appointed — for the falling of some and the rising of others; he is the touchstone that divides, by which men are tried. And he is "a sign spoken against," an object of contradiction and opposition, as his whole history would prove.

So the witnesses make the verse a prophecy of the dividing work of Christ and the opposition he would endure. The lesson the expositors draw is that the coming of the Saviour is no neutral event but the great crisis of souls, by which men either fall or rise. The witnesses note the double sense Barnes draws out: many in Israel would "fall" — stumbling over the lowly Messiah who did not match their dream of a temporal king, rejecting him to their ruin, or being cast down from their proud self-righteousness; and many would "rise" — the humbled lifted up, the self-righteous brought low and then raised to a true righteousness in him. Christ, that is, is "set" as the stone on which some break and by which others are built, the test that reveals and divides the hearts of men (Isaiah 8:14). And he is "a sign spoken against" — destined to be opposed, contradicted, and reviled, as indeed he was, from Herod's malice to the cross. Ambrose's note, the expositors observe, adds that this dividing is also a matter of faith: prophecy and grace are not withheld from the righteous but from unbelievers, who by their own rejection fall. The verse thus sets the infant Saviour forth as the appointed crisis of Israel and the world — the occasion of falling for the proud and unbelieving, of rising for the humble and believing, and himself the sign that all the ages would speak against.
35:
"(Yea, a sword shall pierce through thy own soul also,) that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed." The witnesses dwell on the sword foretold to Mary and divide over its meaning. The grammatical-historical Gill and Barnes take it of her maternal grief at the cross: Gill notes "as he was a man of sorrows, so was she a woman of sorrows, from his cradle to his cross; and his sorrows, like so many darts, rebounded from him to her, and pierced her soul through" — "never more than when she stood at his cross." Barnes: "the sufferings and death of thy Son shall deeply afflict thy soul." A minority, with Lightfoot and Epiphanius, read it of martyrdom: "a prediction that the blessed Virgin should suffer martyrdom... thou shalt die by the sword" — though Lightfoot grants the Scripture is silent and Epiphanius only "gives some countenance" to it.

On "that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed," the witnesses connect it to the foregoing: Christ, the sign spoken against, would by the opposition he aroused lay bare the secret dispositions of men — their faith or their enmity made manifest by their response to him.

So the witnesses make the verse a prophecy of Mary's piercing sorrow and the revealing power of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the personal word to Mary: a "sword" would pierce her soul — and the great body of the witnesses, against the martyrdom-reading, take this of the anguish she would suffer as the mother of the suffering Saviour, above all at the cross, where she would behold her son crucified and feel his agonies as her own. The sorrows of Christ, Gill movingly notes, rebounded upon her throughout his life and reached their height at Calvary; the sword was the grief of a mother watching her child die. The martyrdom-reading of Lightfoot and Epiphanius, the witnesses record but do not press, since Scripture is silent on Mary's death. Second, the purpose clause: the whole work of Christ — his coming, his opposition, the sword — serves "that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed." The witnesses press this as the deepest function of the dividing Christ: by their reception or rejection of him, the secret hearts of men are exposed, faith and unbelief alike brought to light. Mary's own piercing, the expositors observe, is part of this revealing — the cross that wounded her was the cross that uncovered the hearts of all who stood around it. The verse thus joins the mother's sorrow to the Saviour's revealing work, the sword in her soul and the searching of all souls bound together at the cross.
36:
"And there was one Anna, a prophetess, the daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Aser: she was of a great age, and had lived with an husband seven years from her virginity." The witnesses note the prophetess and the fitness of her testimony. Ambrose finds a completeness in the witnesses to Christ: "Simeon had prophesied, a woman united in marriage had prophesied [Elisabeth], a virgin had prophesied [Mary]; it was meet also that a widow should prophesy, that there might lack no sex or condition of life." Theophylact notes the evangelist "dwells some time on the account of Anna, mentioning her father's tribe... adding many witnesses who knew her father and her tribe," and Gregory of Nyssa, that her parentage is given "that there might be a plain way of distinguishing her" from others of the same name.

Origen draws a lesson on widowhood: "the highest blessing is the grace of virginity; but if this cannot be, and a woman lose her husband, let her remain a widow"; Anna was "thought worthy to receive the gift of prophecy, because by long chastity and long fastings she had ascended to this height of virtue."

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction of the second temple-witness to the infant Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the fitness and the holiness of Anna's testimony. Ambrose's observation, the witnesses note, is striking: the coming of Christ is attested by every condition of life — the aged man Simeon, the married Elisabeth, the virgin Mary, and now the widow Anna — that no sex or state should be without its witness to the Saviour. The careful naming of her father and tribe, the expositors observe, lends her testimony weight and credibility, distinguishing this Anna from others and grounding her witness in a known lineage. And her character fits her for the office of prophetess: Origen reads her long widowhood, chastity, and fasting as the discipline by which she rose to the height of virtue that made her a vessel for the Holy Spirit. The witnesses press the pattern: Anna, having lost her husband young, did not seek another marriage but gave herself to a life of devotion, and was rewarded with the gift of prophecy and the privilege of beholding the Christ. The verse thus prepares for her thanksgiving and testimony, presenting her as a holy widow, advanced in age and virtue, fitly chosen to add her voice to Simeon's in proclaiming the redemption come to Israel.
37:
"And she was a widow of about fourscore and four years, which departed not from the temple, but served God with fastings and prayers night and day." The witnesses note Anna's age and her unceasing devotion. The grammatical-historical Gill clarifies the number: "which is not the date of her whole age, as some have thought, but of her widowhood state, as distinct from her marriage state" — so that she had been a widow eighty-four years, of great age indeed. Barnes takes it as her total age, "about eighty-four years," and notes she spent her time "in prayer and in all the ordinances of religion, night and day, continually."

Lightfoot examines "departed not from the temple": he doubts women held any temple office, but Anna "had changed her native soil and fixed her abode at Jerusalem, partly for devotion, that she might be more at leisure for praying in the temple, and partly as a prophetess." Her not departing means she was constant "in the stated times of prayer," ever present at the temple services.

So the witnesses make the verse a portrait of saintly devotion in old age. The lesson the expositors draw is the constancy and fervour of Anna's worship. Whether the eighty-four years measure her widowhood (Gill) or her whole age (Barnes), the witnesses agree she was very old, and had given the long years of her widowhood wholly to God. She "departed not from the temple" — not, the expositors note with Lightfoot, that she literally never left, but that she was unfailingly present at the appointed services, having fixed her dwelling near the temple for the sake of devotion. And she "served God with fastings and prayers night and day" — a life of continual worship, self-denial, and intercession, the very pattern of holy widowhood that Paul would later commend, the widow who "trusteth in God, and continueth in supplications night and day" (1 Timothy 5:5). The witnesses press the example: Anna's old age was not idle nor embittered, but filled with the service of God, her bereavement turned into a calling of prayer and fasting; and her long faithfulness was crowned, as the next verse shows, with the sight of the Redeemer. The verse thus holds up the aged prophetess as a model of persevering devotion — the widow who, denied earthly comfort, found her life in the temple and her joy in waiting on God, until her waiting was rewarded with the Consolation of Israel.
38:
"And she coming in that instant gave thanks likewise unto the Lord, and spake of him to all them that looked for redemption in Jerusalem." The witnesses note the timing of Anna's arrival and the reach of her testimony. The grammatical-historical Clarke marks the moment: "at that very time — while Simeon held the blessed Redeemer in his arms, and was singing his departing song"; and she "as well as Simeon, returned God public thanks for having sent this Saviour to Israel." Gill notes she came "under the impulse and direction of the Spirit of God," as Simeon had.

On "all them that looked for redemption," Clarke explains: "as Daniel's seventy weeks were known to be now completed, the more pious Jews were in constant expectation of the promised Messiah... expecting redemption, such a redemption as was to be brought about by an atonement, or ransom price." On the location, he notes the variant "Israel" for "Jerusalem," and concludes "this widow was one of the first publishers of the Gospel of Christ," perhaps going "from house to house, testifying the grace of God."

So the witnesses make the verse the joining of Anna's voice to Simeon's and the spreading of the good news. The lesson the expositors draw is the testimony of a faithful soul to the coming Redeemer. Anna arrived "in that instant" — by the Spirit's leading, just as Simeon was blessing God over the child — and added her thanksgiving to his, praising God that the Saviour of Israel had come. But she did more than give thanks: she "spake of him" — became a herald of the infant Christ, telling the good news "to all them that looked for redemption." The witnesses note, with Clarke, who these were: the devout remnant who, knowing the prophetic times were fulfilled, waited in expectation for the Messiah and the redemption he would bring — a redemption understood as deliverance through atonement, a ransom for sin. To these waiting saints Anna proclaimed that the long-expected Redeemer had appeared; and in this, the expositors observe, she became one of the first evangelists, carrying the news of Christ to all who hoped for him. The verse thus completes the temple testimony: the aged man and the aged widow together, each moved by the Spirit, bearing witness to the child — Simeon in his song, Anna in her thanksgiving and her preaching — so that the coming of the Saviour, attested in the temple, began to be published abroad to the faithful who awaited the redemption of Israel.
39:
"And when they had performed all things according to the law of the Lord, they returned into Galilee, to their own city Nazareth." The witnesses address the harmonizing of Luke with Matthew's flight into Egypt. Bede explains the omission: "Luke has omitted what he knew to have been sufficiently set forth by Matthew, that the Lord... was carried by His parents into Egypt, and at Herod's death, having returned to Galilee, came to dwell in His own city Nazareth. For the Evangelists individually omit certain things which they know to have been related by others" — so that "by examining another Evangelist, the careful reader may discover the omissions." Augustine reconciles the apparent difficulty: when the angel bade Joseph go "into the land of Israel," it "was at first understood as a command to go into Judea," but finding Archelaus reigning, he withdrew to Galilee, "which might also be understood to include" the land of Israel. The Greek expositor adds that Luke "is here describing the time before the descent to Egypt," before the purification.

Theophylact notes the two cities: "Bethlehem was their paternal city, Nazareth the place of their abode." The witnesses agree the verse, taken with Matthew, gives the full course: presentation, flight to Egypt, return, settlement at Nazareth.

So the witnesses make the verse a case-study in Gospel harmony. The lesson the expositors draw is that the apparent gap between Luke and Matthew is no contradiction but the natural complementarity of independent witnesses. Luke, having recounted the presentation in the temple, passes directly to the return to Nazareth, omitting the flight into Egypt that Matthew records; and the fathers explain, with Bede, that this is the evangelists' regular manner — each omits what he knows another has told, so that no single Gospel is complete in itself, but the whole truth emerges from comparing them. The witnesses note the careful reconciliation: either Luke compresses, leaping over the Egyptian sojourn to the eventual settlement at Nazareth (Bede), or he describes the period before the flight, the presentation having preceded it (the Greek expositor); and Augustine's note resolves how Joseph, directed to "the land of Israel," came to settle in Galilee rather than Judea, the dread of Archelaus turning him northward. The expositors press the lesson against the sceptic: the diversity of the Gospels is not discord but the many-sided testimony of those who, severally, recorded what they knew, leaving to the careful reader the joining of the parts. The verse thus brings the infancy narrative to its resting-place — the holy family settled at Nazareth — and, rightly read with Matthew, displays the harmony of the inspired witnesses.
40:
"And the child grew, and waxed strong in spirit, filled with wisdom: and the grace of God was upon him." The witnesses guard the mystery of Christ's real human growth. The Reformed Gill marks the reality of the body and the manner of the growth: "the child grew in body, in strength, and in stature, which shows it was a true body Christ assumed, and like ours, which did not come to maturity at once"; and he "waxed strong in spirit... as he grew in body, the faculties of his soul opened, and received gradually large measures of wisdom and knowledge." Cyril of Alexandria frames the whole within the incarnation: Christ "of His own accord descended to our poverty, that He might make us rich," and so, truly man, "presented unto the Father, and obedient to the shadows of the law," he passed through real human development.

The witnesses are careful, with Gill, to distinguish: the divine wisdom of the Word, and the fulness of wisdom in him as Mediator, "could not admit of any increase"; it was in his human nature that he grew, the soul of the child receiving by degrees the wisdom that the indwelling Deity and the unmeasured Spirit supplied.

So the witnesses make the verse a confession of the true humanity of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Son of God, taking real flesh, took also a real human development — growing in body, strength, and wisdom as other children do, yet without sin. The witnesses press the careful balance the orthodox faith requires: as God, the Word is unchangeable and all-wise, and could not increase in wisdom; but as man, he truly grew, his human soul and body advancing by natural stages, his faculties opening gradually to receive the wisdom that flowed from his divine nature and the Spirit resting on him without measure. The child was no phantom and no mere appearance of growth, the expositors insist, but genuinely "waxed strong" and was "filled with wisdom," passing through infancy and childhood as we do. Cyril's framing, the witnesses note, sets this within the great condescension: the Rich became poor, the eternal Wisdom became a growing child, that he might sanctify every stage of human life and make us rich by his poverty. "The grace of God was upon him" — the favour and blessing of God resting visibly on the child as he grew. The verse thus holds together the two natures: the true and growing humanity, and the divine fulness that the growth could not increase, in the one Christ who was made like unto us in all things, sin excepted.
41:
"Now his parents went to Jerusalem every year at the feast of the passover." The witnesses note the piety of the holy family and the law's requirement. The grammatical-historical Gill explains the obligation: "Joseph was obliged to go three times a year, as were all the males in Israel, at the feasts of the passover, pentecost, and tabernacles," though "the women were not obliged to go up," the passover of women being "voluntary." Matthew Henry draws out the devotion: "it was their constant practice to attend there, according to the law, though it was a long journey, and they were poor"; "public ordinances must be frequented, and we must not forsake the assembling of ourselves together... worldly business must give way to spiritual concerns."

Henry presses the example: "Joseph and Mary had a son in the house with them that was able to teach them better than all the rabbin at Jerusalem; yet they went up thither, after the custom of the feast. The Lord loves the gates of Zion more than all the dwellings of Jacob, and so should we." Mary, not bound to go, went nonetheless, the witnesses note, out of devotion.

So the witnesses make the verse a model of faithful attendance on God's ordinances. The lesson the expositors draw is the piety of Joseph and Mary, who, though poor and facing a long journey, went up to Jerusalem every year for the passover, as the law required. The witnesses note that the law bound only the men to the three annual feasts, and Mary as a woman was not obliged; yet she too went, her devotion exceeding the bare requirement. Henry's reflection, the expositors press, turns the family's practice into a lesson for the Church: if Joseph and Mary, despite poverty and distance, did not neglect the appointed feasts, how much more should believers prize the public worship of God and not forsake the assembling of themselves together. The most striking touch, the witnesses observe, is Henry's: they had in their own home the Son of God, wiser than all the rabbis of Jerusalem, yet they still went up to the temple after the custom of the feast — for the Lord loves the gates of Zion, and his people are to honour his ordinances even when they might plead other things. The verse thus sets the holy family before us as a pattern of constant, costly devotion to the worship of God, the frame within which the one recorded incident of Christ's boyhood is about to unfold.
42:
"And when he was twelve years old, they went up to Jerusalem after the custom of the feast." The witnesses note the age and find in it both literal and symbolic significance. Cyril ties it to the foregoing: "the Evangelist, having said the Child grew and waxed strong, verifies his words when he relates that Jesus with the holy Virgin went up to Jerusalem." The Greek expositor notes the fitness of the age: "His indication of wisdom did not exceed the measure of His age, but at the time that with us the powers of discernment are generally perfected, the wisdom of Christ shows itself." Bede draws out the humility: "that the Lord came up every year to Jerusalem at the Passover betokens His humility as a man... the Lord as man did among men what God by angels commanded men to do."

Bede and Ambrose offer symbolic readings: Ambrose, that "the twelfth year was the commencement of our Lord's disputation with the doctors, for this was the number of the Evangelists"; Bede, that "by the twelfth number... the universality and perfection of things or times is signified," so that "from the number twelve the glory of Christ takes its beginning."

So the witnesses make the verse the threshold of the one recorded scene of Christ's boyhood. The lesson the expositors draw is the fitness of the age at which his wisdom first openly appeared. At twelve — the age when, among the Jews, a boy became "a son of the law" and his powers of discernment were reckoned to ripen — Jesus went up to the feast, and there, as the next verses tell, his more-than-human understanding began to show. The witnesses note the Greek expositor's careful point: Christ's wisdom did not violate the order of his human growth, appearing prematurely, but manifested itself at the natural time when discernment matures, so that his true humanity is preserved even as his unique wisdom is displayed. Bede's reflection on the annual pilgrimage, the expositors observe, draws out Christ's humility: the Son of God, as man, dutifully did what God required of men, going up to the appointed feast. The symbolic readings of the number twelve — the apostles, the perfection of times — the witnesses record as the fathers' devout meditation, marking the age as the fitting commencement of Christ's manifested glory. The verse thus brings the boy Jesus, at the symbolic and natural threshold of understanding, to Jerusalem and the temple, where his first recorded words would reveal the dawning consciousness of his divine Sonship.
43:
"And when they had fulfilled the days, as they returned, the child Jesus tarried behind in Jerusalem; and Joseph and his mother knew not of it." The witnesses note the duration of the feast and the boy's tarrying. The grammatical-historical Gill explains "the seven days of unleavened bread," which Mary and Joseph, "as did the more religious and devout persons," stayed to keep, though not strictly obliged to remain the whole time. Barnes counts "eight days in all — one day for killing the paschal lamb, and seven for the feast of unleavened bread" (Exodus 12:15; Leviticus 23:5-6). Lightfoot enters into the rabbinic dispute over when one might lawfully depart, concluding the devout stayed the full term.

The Greek expositor (on v.42) had suggested the reason for the tarrying: "that His parents might not be a hindrance to His carrying on the discussion with the lawyers." The witnesses note the parents "knew not of it" — Jesus remained behind without their knowledge, in the press of the departing crowds.

So the witnesses make the verse the beginning of the three days' loss. The lesson the expositors draw, beyond the historical detail of the feast's length, is the deliberate purpose hidden in the boy's tarrying. Joseph and Mary, devout as they were, kept the full days of the feast and then set out homeward; but Jesus "tarried behind in Jerusalem," and they knew it not. The witnesses note the fathers' suggestion that this was no mere childish wandering but a purposed staying, that he might pursue the discourse with the teachers in the temple unhindered. Yet Luke records it simply as a thing the parents did not know — the boy remaining while the company departed. The verse thus sets up the anxious search that follows and the great saying it would draw forth. The careful detail of the feast's days, the expositors observe, grounds the narrative in the real observance of the law; and the parents' ignorance of the tarrying, far from a charge of negligence, reflects the natural circumstances of a great pilgrim crowd, soon to be explained. The boy who "must be about his Father's business" begins here to follow that necessity, tarrying in the temple while his earthly parents, all unknowing, turned toward home — the first hint of the higher claim that would shortly be spoken.
44:
"But they, supposing him to have been in the company, went a day's journey; and they sought him among their kinsfolk and acquaintance." The witnesses explain how the parents could lose the boy without negligence. The grammatical-historical Barnes offers several considerations: "in going to these great feasts, families and neighbours would join together, and form a large collection"; Jesus may have been with them at the start, "seeing him then, they might have been certain of his presence"; and "a part of the company might have left before the others," so that Joseph and Mary "supposed he was with them, until they overtook them at night." Clarke adds that "the men and women marched in separate companies... and the children kept company sometimes with the men, sometimes with the women," which might explain the oversight.

Lightfoot notes the first day's journey from Jerusalem toward Galilee, and the traditional site where the boy was missed. The parents "sought him among their kinsfolk and acquaintance" — the relatives and neighbours who had travelled up with them — before realizing he was not in the caravan at all.

So the witnesses make the verse an explanation of the loss and a picture of the parents' dawning anxiety. The lesson the expositors draw is that the loss of Jesus, far from a sign of carelessness, arose naturally from the circumstances of the pilgrim caravan. The witnesses press, with Barnes and Clarke, the reasonable account: great companies of families and neighbours travelled together; the men and women, and the children among them, moved in separate groups; and Joseph and Mary, each perhaps supposing the boy was with the other or among the kindred, journeyed a full day before missing him. Only at evening, when the company gathered and he was nowhere found, did they begin to search among their relatives and acquaintances. The expositors note the tender realism: the parents are not blamed, for the conditions of the journey made the mistake easy; yet their search, beginning among the known and near, would soon turn to anxious retracing of their steps. The verse thus carries forward the narrative of the three days' loss, showing the parents' confidence giving way to concern as they seek the boy first among their own, before the dreadful realization that he had been left behind in Jerusalem. The scene prepares for the sorrowing search and the reunion in the temple, where the boy's answer would lift the whole incident from a family's distress to a revelation of his higher Sonship.
45:
"And when they found him not, they turned back again to Jerusalem, seeking him." The witnesses note the parents' anxious return. The grammatical-historical Gill states it plainly: "when they found him not in the company that came from Jerusalem, nor among any of their relations and friends, with whom they supposed he was," they turned back. Clarke and Robertson press the intensity of the search: the better text reads anazētountes, "seeking him diligently," and Clarke reasons "if they sought earnestly when they first found him missing, their solicitude and diligence must be greatly increased during his three days' absence." Robertson notes the present participle marks the continuing search — "this was all that was worth while now, finding the lost boy."

The parents, the witnesses agree, retraced the whole day's journey back to the city, their anxiety mounting with every step, searching as they went for the child they had lost.

So the witnesses make the brief verse a picture of anxious and diligent seeking. The lesson the expositors draw is the earnestness with which the lost Christ is to be sought. Joseph and Mary, having failed to find the boy in the caravan or among their kindred, did not despair or delay but "turned back again to Jerusalem, seeking him" — undoing the day's journey, retracing every step, their hearts full of solicitude for the missing child. The witnesses note, with Clarke and Robertson, the force of the word: not a casual looking but a diligent, anxious search, intensified by the days of his absence, which would consume all their thought until he was found. The expositors have often drawn from this the spiritual application: that the soul which has lost the sense of Christ's presence must seek him again with like diligence — retracing its steps, returning to where he was last known, not resting till he is found. The parents' immediate turning back, the witnesses press, shows the proper response to the loss of so great a treasure: no leisurely inquiry, but an urgent and wholehearted search. The verse thus carries the narrative toward its resolution, the sorrowing parents hastening back to the city where, after three days, they would find the boy in his Father's house — the diligent seeking rewarded, as it ever is, with the finding of the Christ.
46:
"And it came to pass, that after three days they found him in the temple, sitting in the midst of the doctors, both hearing them, and asking them questions." The witnesses reckon the three days and dwell on the scene among the teachers. The grammatical-historical Gill counts them: "the first day was spent in journeying, the second in coming back the same journey, and the third they sought all Jerusalem for him." Barnes agrees: "on the third day after they had left Jerusalem." Lightfoot enters into the temple's layout — the courts, the synagogue "near the court, in the Mountain of the Temple" — and the rabbinic custom of teaching, noting that after Gamaliel's death disciples "learned the law sitting," whereas earlier they stood; he wonders what place the boy could have held "amongst the doctors."

The witnesses note the boy's posture and activity: "sitting in the midst of the doctors, both hearing them, and asking them questions." He was not teaching as a master, but in the place of a learner — yet hearing and questioning with a discernment that, the next verse shows, astonished all.

So the witnesses make the verse the finding of the boy Jesus among the teachers of the law. The lesson the expositors draw is the picture of the child's holy thirst for the things of God and his more-than-natural understanding. After three days of anxious search, the parents found him not playing nor lost in the streets, but in the temple — his Father's house — "in the midst of the doctors," the learned rabbis who expounded the law. The witnesses note carefully his posture: he sat, hearing them and asking them questions, in the manner of a diligent and eager scholar, not assuming the seat of a teacher. Yet, as the next verse will declare, his hearing and his questions revealed an understanding far beyond his years. Lightfoot's antiquarian detail, the expositors observe, fills in the setting — the temple courts and synagogue where the doctors taught, the custom of sitting at instruction — and underlines how remarkable it was that a boy of twelve should be found in such company, holding his own among the masters. The scene, the witnesses press, shows the boy Jesus already drawn to the law and the worship of God, his human mind eagerly engaging the deepest questions, while the wisdom of his divine nature shone through his words. The verse thus reveals where the lost Christ was found — in the temple, among the teachers, about his Father's business — and prepares for the astonishment of the doctors and the saying that would unveil his Sonship.
47:
"And all that heard him were astonished at his understanding and answers." The witnesses note the amazement of the learned at the boy's discernment. The grammatical-historical Robertson describes the scene vividly: "were amazed (imperfect, descriptive of their continued and repeated astonishment)... they stood out of themselves as if their eyes were bulging out. The boy had a holy thirst for knowledge, and he used a boy's way of learning." On "understanding," he notes it is "the grasp and comprehension, comparing and combining things." Ellicott observes that "answers" may include "the discernment which showed itself in the questions as well as the answers," and cites Josephus, who boasted that at fourteen "the chief priests used to consult him" — showing "the class of teachers retained an interest in quick and promising scholars."

Clarke notes the word for "answers" may cover "what Jesus said by way of question to the doctors," questions and answers alike revealing his wisdom. The doctors, Gill says, "both the doctors and their disciples, were amazed."

So the witnesses make the verse the testimony of the learned to the boy's surpassing wisdom. The lesson the expositors draw is the early manifestation of Christ's divine understanding, shining through his human childhood. The doctors of the law — men learned in the Scriptures and accustomed to able pupils — were not merely pleased but "astonished," the witnesses note, their amazement continuing and repeated as the boy's discernment unfolded. What struck them was his "understanding and answers": the grasp and penetration of his questions and replies, far beyond what his years could explain. Robertson's vivid rendering, the expositors observe, captures the scene — the teachers beside themselves with wonder at the child's comprehension. Ellicott's comparison with Josephus, who at fourteen drew the consultation of the priests, sets the natural context: the rabbis prized quick scholars; but the boy Jesus exceeded even this, his wisdom being not the precocity of a clever child only, but the shining-through of the divine wisdom that dwelt in him. The witnesses are careful, here as at verse 40, to keep the balance: he used "a boy's way of learning," hearing and asking, his human mind truly engaging; yet what came forth astonished the masters, for in him the eternal Wisdom was made flesh. The verse thus marks the first public token of who this child was — his understanding amazing the very teachers of Israel — and sets the stage for the still greater wonder of his answer to his mother.
48:
"And when they saw him, they were amazed: and his mother said unto him, Son, why hast thou thus dealt with us? behold, thy father and I have sought thee sorrowing." The witnesses note the parents' astonishment and Mary's gentle reproach. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the strength of the word: "they were struck out by what they saw and heard. Even they had not fully realized the power in this wonderful boy. Parents often fail to perceive the wealth of nature in their children." On Mary's "thy father," Barnes explains: "Joseph was not the real father of Jesus, but he was legally so; and as the secret of his birth was not commonly known, he was called his father; Mary, in accordance with that usage, also called him so." Clarke gently notes: "it certainly was not his fault, but theirs; men are very apt to lay on others the blame of their own misconduct."

The Reformed Calvin reads Mary's words not as authority but as grief: "this complaint was not the result of ambition, but the expression of grief, which had lasted three days." Ellicott notes the continuous tense — "we were seeking thee."

So the witnesses make the verse the reunion and the mother's sorrowful question. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of the parents' anxiety and the limits of even Mary's understanding. Finding the boy at last among the doctors, Joseph and Mary were "amazed" — struck, Robertson notes, by the scene they beheld, for even they had not fully grasped the wonder of their child. And Mary, out of three days' grief, gently reproaches him: "Son, why hast thou thus dealt with us?" The witnesses note the tender realism of her words — the anxious mother who had sought her child "sorrowing," fearing some harm had befallen him. Her calling Joseph "thy father," the expositors carefully observe (as at v.33), follows the common usage by which Joseph, legally and by repute the boy's father, was so named, the secret of the virgin birth not being publicly known; Mary speaks as others would, not denying the mystery she knew. Calvin's reading guards her words from any charge of pride: she does not assert maternal authority but pours out a grief long pent up. The witnesses press the contrast that the next verse will sharpen: the mother speaks of "thy father and I," thinking of the earthly household and its anxiety; the boy will answer by pointing to his heavenly Father and the higher claim upon him. The verse thus joins the parents' relief and reproach to the threshold of the great saying, in which the human family's sorrow meets the dawning consciousness of the Son of God.
49:
"And he said unto them, How is it that ye sought me? wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business?" The witnesses dwell on Christ's first recorded words and a question of translation. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the gentle correction: "Mary should have known that the Son of God was safe... that his heavenly Father would take care of him." On "my Father's business," he notes "some think this should be translated in my Father's house — that is, in the temple," and presses the deeper sense: "Jesus reminded them that he came down from heaven; that he had a higher Father than an earthly parent; and that even in early life it was proper that he should be engaged in the work for which he came" (John 9:4). Clarke gives both renderings — "My Father's concerns," or "in my Father's house" — the latter explaining why they should have sought him in the temple: "where should a child be found, but in his father's house?"

The witnesses note the gentle reproof in "How is it that ye sought me?" — and the great revelation in "my Father," set pointedly over against Mary's "thy father." Jesus claims God as his Father in a unique sense, and a necessity ("I must") laid upon him by that relation.

So the witnesses make the verse the first unveiling of Christ's divine self-consciousness. The lesson the expositors draw is the dawning, even in boyhood, of his knowledge of his unique Sonship and mission. To Mary's "thy father and I," Jesus answers with "my Father" — gently lifting their eyes from Joseph to God, and claiming a relation to the Father that was his alone. The witnesses note the tender reproof: "How is it that ye sought me?" — as if to say, you should have known where to find me, and that I was safe in my Father's care. The famous phrase, whether rendered "about my Father's business" or "in my Father's house," the expositors hold to the same effect: the boy declares that he belongs to God his Father, and that a necessity — "I must" — rests upon him to be occupied with his Father's concerns, in his Father's house. The witnesses press the wonder Robertson notes (on v.50): here, for the first time, Jesus calls God "Father," not "Master," marking a new era and the consciousness of his divine origin. The "must," the expositors observe, is the first of the many divine necessities that would govern his life and lead him to the cross. The verse thus reveals the twelve-year-old already aware, in some measure, of who he was and what he came to do — the Son of God, claimed by his Father's house and his Father's business, even before his public ministry began.
50:
"And they understood not the saying which he spake unto them." The witnesses note the parents' failure to grasp his words and address the apparent tension with the earlier revelations. The grammatical-historical Barnes finds it telling: "it is remarkable that they did not understand Jesus in this, but it shows how slow persons are to believe; even his parents... did not seem to comprehend that he was to be the Saviour of men, or understood it very imperfectly." Robertson presses the depth: "even Mary, with all her previous preparation and brooding, was not equal to the dawning of the Messianic consciousness in her boy... a new era has come when Jesus calls God Father, not Master. Even we do not yet fully understand what Jesus the boy here said."

Ellicott answers the sceptic who sees a contradiction with the annunciation: "twelve years of childhood after the outward pattern of that of other children may have dulled the impressions then made; and even if they understood the words as referring to the marvel of his birth, they were still in the dark as to what he meant by being about his Father's business... it is clear the writer was not conscious of any inconsistency."

So the witnesses make the verse a window into the mystery of Christ's growing self-revelation and his parents' limited understanding. The lesson the expositors draw is the slowness of even the godliest to comprehend the things of God. Joseph and Mary, who had heard the angel, the shepherds, Simeon, and Anna, yet "understood not the saying" — they did not grasp the full meaning of the boy's claim to a unique Sonship and a divine necessity. The witnesses note, with Robertson, the profundity of what had been spoken: the dawning of the Messianic consciousness, the boy calling God his Father, opened a depth that even Mary's long pondering could not fathom. The expositors answer the objection that this contradicts the earlier revelations: years of an outwardly ordinary childhood, Ellicott explains, may have dimmed the first impressions, and even if the parents recalled the wonders of his birth, the particular meaning of "my Father's business" lay beyond them. There is no inconsistency, the witnesses press, but the natural truth that understanding grows by degrees, and that the deepest things of Christ are apprehended slowly, even by those closest to him. The verse thus humbly records the limit of the parents' insight — a momentary flash of more-than-human greatness, not yet grasped, fading back (as Ellicott says) into the light of common day — and reminds the reader that the mystery of the Son of God outruns even the comprehension of his mother.
51:
"And he went down with them, and came to Nazareth, and was subject unto them: but his mother kept all these sayings in her heart." The witnesses dwell on Christ's submission and Mary's treasuring. The Greek expositor sums up the hidden years: "all that time of the life of Christ which He passed between His manifestation in the temple and His baptism, being devoid of any great public miracles, the Evangelist sums up in one word, saying, And he went down with them." Origen draws lessons from the submission: "Jesus the Son of God is subject to Joseph and Mary... let us then ourselves be subject to our parents"; and he marvels, "it seems that Joseph knew Jesus was greater than he, and therefore in awe moderated his authority — oftentimes he who is subject is the greater." The Greek expositor notes Christ "shows forth by His work these three things: the love of God, honour to parents, but the preferring God also to our parents" — for "when blamed by His parents He counts all of less moment than what belongs to God; again, He gives His obedience to His parents."

Bede presses the example: "what is the teacher of virtue, unless he fulfil his duty to his parents? What else did He do among us, than what He wished should be done by us?" Mary, the witnesses note, "kept all these sayings" — pondering the mystery she could not yet fathom.

So the witnesses make the verse a portrait of the obedient Christ and the meditating mother. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the humility and obedience of the Son of God, who, having just declared his higher Sonship, "went down with them" to Nazareth and "was subject unto them." The witnesses note the wonder the fathers feel: the Lord of all, greater than his parents, willingly submitted to Joseph and Mary, sanctifying by his example the honour and obedience children owe their parents. Origen's reflection deepens it — that the truly greater may rightly be subject to the lesser, and that those in authority should learn humility from a Christ who obeyed those beneath him. Yet the witnesses note the careful order the Greek expositor draws: Christ preferred God to his parents (in the temple, counting his Father's business above their will), yet honoured his parents (in Nazareth, yielding them obedience) — the two held in their right relation, God first, then parents for God's sake. Second, Mary's treasuring: she "kept all these sayings in her heart," pondering the words and wonders she did not yet understand, storing them up for the day when their meaning would be clear. The verse thus closes the boyhood narrative with the double note of Christ's perfect obedience and Mary's faithful meditation — the Son submitting, the mother pondering — through the long hidden years at Nazareth.
52:
"And Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man." The witnesses return to the mystery of Christ's growth, now in his maturing years. The Reformed Gill repeats the careful distinction: Jesus "increased in wisdom as man; for neither his divine wisdom, nor the treasures of wisdom and knowledge in him as mediator, could admit of any increase; but as he grew in body, the faculties of his soul opened, and received gradually large measures of wisdom and knowledge... through the indwelling of his divine nature and the Holy Spirit that was, without measure, on him." Barnes glosses "favour with God": "this does not imply that he ever lacked the favour of God, but that God regarded him with favour in proportion as he showed an understanding and spirit like his own."

Barnes draws the practical lesson: "happy are those children who imitate the example of Jesus — who are obedient to parents, who increase in wisdom, who are sober, temperate, and industrious, and who thus increase in favour with God and men" (1 Samuel 2:26).

So the witnesses make the verse the summary of Christ's hidden years and the confession, once more, of his true humanity. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality and the manner of his fourfold growth — in wisdom, in stature, in favour with God, and in favour with man. The witnesses press, as at verse 40, the orthodox care the words require: as God, the Son is unchangeably all-wise and could not increase; but as man, he truly grew, his body advancing in stature and his human soul opening by degrees to receive the wisdom that his indwelling Deity and the unmeasured Spirit supplied. He "increased in favour with God" — not that he ever lacked it, the expositors are careful to say, but that the favour of God rested ever more visibly upon him as his human excellence unfolded; and "with man," as his wisdom and grace won the esteem of those around him. The witnesses note the parallel with the child Samuel, who likewise "grew in favour both with the Lord and with men" (1 Samuel 2:26). Barnes's application, the expositors observe, turns the verse to the nurture of the young: the children who imitate the boy Jesus — obedient, growing in wisdom, sober and diligent — themselves increase in favour with God and man. The verse thus closes the infancy and boyhood narrative with the quiet record of Christ's perfect human development through the silent years at Nazareth — the eternal Wisdom growing as a man, advancing in grace and favour, until the hour of his showing to Israel should come.
